unit.name: Palletizer
unit.cfj: Stack and arrange cased goods onto pallets in defined load patterns for shipment

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified load patterns allow cases to shift or fall during transfer or shipment
rows.plain: Choose a way to arrange and secure the load so cases do not move or fall during handling or shipping. This helps prevent damage and accidents.
rows.imp_rat: Load patterns allowing cases to shift or fall during transfer/shipment is a direct stability failure central to the CFJ; falling cases cause damage and injury.
rows.sat_rat: Standard wrapping and pattern rules reduce collapse but transit shifting still occurs with meaningful frequency. Load containment checks happen before shipment but shifting during transit is often only discovered on arrival. Predicting shift and fall behavior needs specialized load-securement knowledge not always present on the floor. Stretch wrap and dunnage are cheap, but returned or damaged loads impose significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unstable palletized load collapsing onto personnel during handling, storage, or transport
rows.plain: Prevent a poorly balanced stack of products from falling onto people while it is moved, stored, or transported. This helps prevent serious injuries.
rows.imp_rat: An unstable load collapsing onto personnel is a catastrophic safety failure directly tied to the CFJ of producing stable shipment-ready loads; if this occurs the job has failed outright and lives are at risk.
rows.sat_rat: Established load patterns, stretch wrapping and warehouse safety rules make collapses onto personnel relatively rare, but intermittent failures still occur when patterns or cases vary. Load stability issues are usually caught before shipment during visual inspection, though catching them takes ongoing attention over the whole run. Recognizing an unstable load and preventing collapse depends heavily on experienced operator judgement about tier patterns and interlock. Stretch wrap, slip sheets and corner boards are inexpensive standard mitigations widely deployed for this risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unstable completed load is accepted for shipment
rows.plain: Prevent a completed load that could fall over from being approved for shipment. This helps protect people and products during transport.
rows.imp_rat: An unstable completed load accepted for shipment is the terminal stability gate failure central to the CFJ; the escaped defect risks collapse, damage, and injury in transit.
rows.sat_rat: There is little automated stability verification, so unstable loads are inconsistently caught before shipment. Stability judgement rarely adds delay but a bad call cascades downstream. Assessing stability still leans heavily on operator eyeball judgement. An unstable load accepted for shipment risks collapse, damage claims and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an unstable completed load as stable before discharge
rows.plain: Do not remove a completed load until it is confirmed to be stable. This helps prevent damaged or unsafe items from being moved onward.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an unstable load as stable before discharge is a gatekeeping failure central to shipping stable loads; the accepted defect propagates into transport with high stakes.
rows.sat_rat: There is no widely deployed objective stability measurement, so unstable loads can pass verification and fail later, leaving this poorly served. Stability judgement is often a quick visual check or downstream stretch-wrap step that adds little time. Deciding whether a completed load is truly stable still leans heavily on operator experience or crude visual inspection. Accepting an unstable load that collapses in transit is costly, and status-quo checks catch only obvious cases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that load patterns vulnerable to case shifting or collapse are judged safe
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a stack or arrangement of loads that could shift or fall is judged safe. This matters to prevent damage, spills, or injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Judging unstable-vulnerable patterns as safe defeats the stability assurance essential to the CFJ; passing collapse-prone loads risks damage and injury downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Misclassifying vulnerable patterns as safe is a recurring failure because validation practice is inconsistent. Stability judgements are made at assessment time and rarely revisited before shipment, allowing stale conclusions. Judging whether a load pattern is truly stable relies heavily on expert intuition rather than validated modeling. Load-stability testing equipment is costly, so many rely on visual judgement instead.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified load patterns produce unstable unit loads during palletizer discharge, handling, storage, or shipment
rows.plain: Avoid choosing a stacking arrangement that makes packages unstable while they are released, moved, stored, or shipped. Stable stacks help prevent damage and handling accidents.
rows.imp_rat: Load patterns producing unstable unit loads is central to the CFJ of stable stacking; instability during discharge/transport risks collapse, damage, and injury—high stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Mature palletizing software and validated pattern libraries catch most instability, but real-world discharge and transit still surprise loads with unusual cases. Pattern libraries and palletizing software return stable patterns quickly, so evaluating stability adds little delay. Standard stability rules and software heuristics reduce but do not eliminate the need for experienced judgement on marginal loads. Existing pattern tools are already amortized, so avoiding unstable patterns costs little beyond occasional trial runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unstable cased-goods stack collapses during palletizing or pallet transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an unstable stack of boxed products falls while being placed on or moved with a pallet. This helps protect people, products, and equipment.
rows.imp_rat: An unstable stack collapsing during palletizing or transfer is a core stability failure with in-process collapse; consequences include product damage and worker injury.
rows.sat_rat: Proven load patterns keep stacks stable in normal operation, but jams, uneven layers, and gripper faults still trigger collapses during transfer. Stacking proceeds at machine pace with no added delay for stability. Automated interlocking patterns embed stability, lessening the need for skilled manual stacking. A mid-build collapse scraps cases and stops the line, incurring downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of pallets with load-pattern violations released without detection
rows.plain: Reduce the number of pallets with incorrectly arranged loads that are sent out without anyone noticing. This helps keep loads stable and safe during handling and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Load-pattern violations released undetected defeats verification and lets nonconforming pallets ship, central to load-pattern conformance; downstream rejection results.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely a reliable end-of-line pattern-verification gate, so violated pallets are released without detection more often than the overseer would like. Detection today usually happens at intermittent visual spot-checks rather than in-line, so violations may not be caught until later. Recognizing a subtle load-pattern violation depends heavily on an attentive, experienced checker rather than an automated gate. Undetected violations reach shipping and can trigger returns or rejected loads, an expensive downstream failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a density-optimized load shifts or topples during expected handling and shipment
rows.plain: Pack the items densely without allowing the load to shift or fall during normal handling and shipping. This helps protect the items and the people handling them.
rows.imp_rat: A density-optimized load toppling during handling/shipment is a direct stability failure of the core stacking job; consequences include product loss and safety risk.
rows.sat_rat: The inherent tension between packing density and stability means status-quo tools often push loads to the edge of failing under real handling. Density optimization tools produce candidate patterns quickly without much added evaluation time. Balancing density against stability during handling demands scarce expertise that most operations lack in depth. Density gains are pursued for cost savings, but resulting shift/topple losses partly offset those savings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a palletized load shifts or collapses before leaving the palletizing area
rows.plain: Keep a stacked pallet load from shifting or falling before it leaves the packing area. This protects the products and keeps people nearby safe.
rows.imp_rat: A load shifting/collapsing before leaving the palletizing area is a direct stability failure of the CFJ; collapse causes product loss and immediate safety risk.
rows.sat_rat: Interlock/pattern design and prompt wrapping make in-area collapse uncommon, though tall or light loads still occasionally shift before wrapping. Stretch-wrapping and immediate securing happen in-line with negligible added time. Stable stacking is largely engineered into the pattern, reducing dependence on operator skill. A load that shifts or collapses in-area wastes the whole pallet plus cleanup labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the evaluation criteria accept an arrangement prone to load shift, case damage, or pallet overhang
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the selection rules approve a setup where loads can move, boxes can be damaged, or goods hang over the edge of a pallet. This helps keep stored and moved goods stable and undamaged.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation criteria accepting arrangements prone to shift/damage/overhang lets defective loads pass verification, central to ensuring shippable loads; consequences include field failures.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria are often informal or inherited, so they inconsistently reject arrangements prone to shift, damage, or overhang. Criteria checks are typically fast once a checklist or software rule set exists. Judging whether criteria adequately screen for shift, damage, and overhang still leans heavily on experienced specifiers. Poorly set criteria that accept bad arrangements drive rework and damage costs that are only partly contained today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified load patterns require worker entry into crush or pinch zones during stacking or recovery
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that stacking or recovering loads requires workers to enter areas where they could be crushed or pinched. This helps make the work safer.
rows.imp_rat: Patterns requiring worker entry into crush/pinch zones is a serious safety hazard, but it is a design/handling concern peripheral to the core stacking function which can proceed automated.
rows.sat_rat: Standard machine-guarding standards address obvious zones but foreseeable stacking and recovery entries are frequently missed. Crush and pinch zone identification is typically a one-time design review, leaving delays before real entry needs are re-examined. Anticipating which patterns force worker entry into pinch zones demands experienced safety judgement that is not uniformly available. Guarding and pattern redesign to avoid entry zones adds capital cost that pressures the assessment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unsafe manual handling during jams, misalignments, damaged-case events, or partial-load recovery
rows.plain: Reduce the need for people to handle equipment or packages in unsafe ways when something gets stuck, does not line up, is damaged, or only partly loaded. This helps prevent injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Unsafe manual handling during jams/misalignments/recovery is a real injury risk during interventions, but these are exception events peripheral to normal automated stacking.
rows.sat_rat: Unsafe manual handling during jams remains a chronic recurring problem despite lockout procedures. Jam and recovery events happen unpredictably, and safe procedures are often improvised on the spot rather than pre-timed. Safely clearing jams and damaged cases requires trained operators, and reliance on individual judgement is high. Manual recovery consumes labor and causes downtime, adding operational cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pinch, crush, or struck-by exposure during foreseeable load correction is overlooked
rows.plain: Make sure possible risks of pinching, crushing, or being hit are noticed when someone has to correct a load. This helps protect people who adjust or fix the load.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking pinch/crush/struck-by exposure during load correction is a safety assessment gap; relevant to safe operation but peripheral to the automated stacking function, though injury severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Correction scenarios are inconsistently covered, so these exposures are frequently overlooked. Foreseeing correction hazards is a discrete analysis step that adds little recurring delay. Anticipating pinch, crush, and struck-by exposure during load correction demands scarce foresight that assessors often lack. The analysis itself is low cost but overlooked hazards drive expensive incidents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the palletizer cannot execute a specified pattern within its case-handling and placement constraints
rows.plain: Make sure the pallet-stacking machine can arrange cases in the requested pattern while staying within its handling and placement limits. This helps the load remain stable and fit as intended.
rows.imp_rat: The palletizer being unable to execute a specified pattern within its constraints is a fundamental capability failure of the core stacking job; the job simply cannot be done.
rows.sat_rat: Palletizer capability limits are generally known to programmers, so infeasible specs are usually caught before production though edge cases slip through. Machine constraint checks are quick when the palletizer's capability envelope is documented. Matching pattern geometry to a specific palletizer's grip and placement limits needs equipment-specific expertise. Unexecutable patterns cause downtime and reprogramming costs that current documentation partly avoids.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of potentially unstable or rejectable pallet loads released before issue closure
rows.plain: Do not release pallets that may be unstable or unacceptable until the problem is resolved. This helps prevent unsafe or damaged loads from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing potentially unstable/rejectable loads before issue closure lets defects escape, undermining stable-load assurance; consequences include downstream rejection and hazard.
rows.sat_rat: Without a firm release gate tied to issue closure, potentially unstable loads frequently slip out under production pressure. Holding a suspect load pending closure delays shipment, and pressure to ship often overrides thorough verification. Judging whether a questionable load is truly shippable before release depends on scarce experienced judgement rather than a clear gate. Releasing marginal loads that later reject costs return handling, but holding them costs storage and delay too.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of case placement or orientation deviations remaining undetected during load verification
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a case is loaded in the wrong position or direction without anyone noticing during the check. This matters because the load may not meet requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Placement/orientation deviations remaining undetected during verification lets pattern defects escape, central to conformance assurance; consequences are downstream nonconformance.
rows.sat_rat: Without vision-based verification, deviations frequently escape manual checks, so this need is served only moderately. Load verification is usually a manual visual pass that adds inspection time without catching everything. Spotting placement or orientation deviations depends heavily on inspector vigilance and pattern familiarity. Missed deviations propagate to shipping errors and rework, a persistent cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of cased goods crushed, deformed, or opened by the specified stacking arrangement
rows.plain: Use a stacking arrangement that does not crush, bend, or open the boxes. This protects the products while they are stored or moved.
rows.imp_rat: Cases crushed/deformed/opened by the stacking arrangement is a direct product-integrity failure of the pattern design, central to producing shippable goods; damage causes loss.
rows.sat_rat: Established stacking rules catch gross errors, yet the actual crushed/opened rate remains a recurring, imperfectly controlled failure. Damage-rate checks against a pattern are quick when data exists. Predicting which arrangements open or deform cases requires experienced review of case quality and pattern interaction. Crushed or opened cases are direct product and rework losses that current practice reduces but does not eliminate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of pallets produced during recovery that violate the specified load pattern
rows.plain: Keep recovered shipments within the required arrangement when they are placed on pallets. Incorrect stacking can make loads unsafe or damage the products.
rows.imp_rat: Pallets built during recovery violating the load pattern extends nonconformance into exception operations, relevant to sustained conformance; moderate escaped-defect consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Post-disruption startup is a well-known weak spot where pattern violations spike and few safeguards specifically target this state. During recovery, operators are rushing to restore output, so conformance checks are often shortcut. Building correct patterns amid a disruption demands strong operator judgement precisely when attention is stretched thin. Recovery-period pallets that violate the pattern add rework and scrap on top of the original downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed pallets containing cases outside specified positions or orientations
rows.plain: Have fewer finished pallets with boxes in the wrong places or facing the wrong way. This helps pallets remain stable and fit the expected handling process.
rows.imp_rat: Completed pallets with cases outside specified positions/orientations is the terminal nonconformance of the arrangement job; consequences include rejection and instability downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Programmed layer patterns give high positional consistency in normal running, yet slipped cases, jams, and worn tooling still produce out-of-position cases that go uncaught. Automated palletizers place cases in defined patterns continuously without added cycle time, so throughput is unaffected by pattern conformance. Mechanical placement removes most manual finesse, though initial recipe setup and misfeed correction still lean on skilled technicians. Occasional restacking and rejected pallets consume labor and material but are a modest fraction of output.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of case loads exceeding carton strength during stacking
rows.plain: Prevent stacked boxes from carrying more weight than they can safely support. Too much weight can damage the boxes and the products inside.
rows.imp_rat: Case loads exceeding carton strength during stacking causes crushing and collapse, central to safe stable stacking; consequences are product damage and instability.
rows.sat_rat: Established stacking specs generally keep loads within strength, but variable carton quality still causes occasional failures. Stacking to strength limits is governed by fixed patterns so no per-load time is spent judging it. Load-height and layer limits are engineered into the pattern, reducing reliance on operator knowledge of carton strength. Exceeding carton strength crushes product, a costly loss when patterns are wrong or cases weaken.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of damaged cases that leave palletizing without detection
rows.plain: Detect more damaged boxes before they leave the pallet-building area. This helps prevent damaged products from being sent onward.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged cases leaving palletizing undetected lets defective product ship, relevant to output quality; consequences are downstream rejection and customer impact, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Without inline damage detection, defective cases regularly leave palletizing undetected, especially subtle damage on inner-layer cases. Damage checks are done by eye during handling, adding little time but easily skipped under pace. Spotting damaged cases relies on operator attentiveness rather than automated inspection. Damaged cases reaching customers cause returns and complaints, a significant quality cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified unit loads cannot pass through downstream handling, storage, or transport interfaces
rows.plain: Ensure that the specified pallet loads can pass through later handling, storage, and transport equipment and spaces. This helps prevent delays, damage, or the need to repack them.
rows.imp_rat: Unit loads unable to pass downstream handling/storage/transport interfaces is a serious functional failure tied to shippability; such loads must be rebuilt, causing major disruption.
rows.sat_rat: Standard pallet and trailer dimensions are well known, but customer-specific and storage interfaces still cause occasional unexpected rejections. Checking against known rack, door, and trailer dimensions is a quick lookup once documented. Knowing every downstream interface constraint requires distributed knowledge that a single specifier rarely holds fully. Loads that cannot pass interfaces cause costly rehandling and rejections that are only partly prevented.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case dimensional variation prevents the specified arrangement from fitting
rows.plain: Make sure differences in the sizes of cases or containers do not prevent them from fitting in the required arrangement. This helps use space efficiently without making the planned layout impossible.
rows.imp_rat: Case dimensional variation preventing the specified arrangement from fitting directly blocks correct pattern execution central to the CFJ; consequences are jams and misbuilds.
rows.sat_rat: Case dimensional variation is frequently underspecified, so patterns that fit nominal cases still fail on real production variation. Re-checking fit against dimensional variation adds modest time when tolerances are known. Anticipating how case dimension drift breaks a pattern requires attentive judgement often missing at specification time. Misfits cause line stoppages and rework whose cost is only partly avoided today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of in-scope case configurations lacking a pattern that satisfies density, stability, and shipment constraints
rows.plain: Make sure each approved box arrangement has a workable way to pack products closely while keeping them stable and suitable for shipping. This helps use space well without risking damage.
rows.imp_rat: Case configurations lacking any pattern that satisfies density/stability/shipment constraints means the job cannot be validly done for those goods, a fundamental capability gap central to the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful minority of case configurations still lack a pattern that jointly meets density, stability, and shipment constraints. Finding a satisfying pattern for a given case is fast for common sizes but slow for awkward ones. Configurations lacking any good pattern demand expert intervention that is not always available. Configurations without viable patterns force costly compromises on density or stability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unsupported case overhang beyond pallet edges in completed loads
rows.plain: Reduce the amount that finished loads extend unsupported beyond the edges of a pallet. This helps keep the load stable and prevents goods from being damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported overhang beyond pallet edges directly degrades load stability and downstream fit, central to the arrangement job; overhang risks collapse and handling damage.
rows.sat_rat: Squaring plates and pattern control limit overhang fairly consistently, though soft or oversized cases still overhang at times. Overhang forms during normal stacking and detecting it does not add meaningful time when squaring devices are present. Load squarers and programmed patterns reduce reliance on operator eyeballing of edge alignment. Overhang leads to crushed edges and rejected loads, a recurring but partially contained cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of partial-load shifts during case placement on the pallet
rows.plain: Prevent boxes or other goods from shifting when they are placed on the pallet. This helps keep the load stable and properly arranged.
rows.imp_rat: Partial-load shifts during case placement is an in-process stability failure central to building stable loads; shifts cause misplacement, damage, and collapse risk.
rows.sat_rat: Good patterns and stabilizers limit shifts, but slick or uneven cases still cause intermittent movement. Partial-load shifts halt building and require re-squaring, costing time. Preventing shifts during build relies on pattern design more than operator finesse on automated lines. Shifts cause damaged or rebuilt loads, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of case placements outside specified load positions during pallet building
rows.plain: Place fewer boxes outside the required positions when building a pallet load. This helps keep the load stable and arranged correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Case placement outside specified positions strikes at the heart of arranging goods in defined load patterns; misplacement produces nonconforming, potentially unstable loads with moderate downstream consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Programmed pattern control and mechanical guides make in-position placement highly consistent, making this one of the better-served needs today. Automated palletizers place cases in programmed positions cycle after cycle without added time cost, though occasional misplacements trigger short stoppages. Modern robotic and layer-forming palletizers encode load patterns so operators rarely need manual placement judgement once a recipe is set. Misplaced cases occasionally cause rework or damaged loads, but the incidence with programmed systems is low enough to keep waste modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of replenished load bases with structural damage at the load-forming point
rows.plain: Reduce damage to the platforms used to build loads when new platforms are supplied. This helps keep the loads stable and safe to handle.
rows.imp_rat: Replenished load bases with structural damage at load forming compromises the base of the stack, relevant to stable-load integrity; damaged pallets risk collapse in transit.
rows.sat_rat: Broken pallets pass through with poor detection, so damaged bases regularly reach the load-forming point. A damaged base found during forming causes a swap and delay. Detecting structural pallet damage depends on operator vigilance. Damaged bases cause load collapse and product loss if missed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of out-of-specification load height or overhang remaining undetected during conformance verification
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of failing to notice that a load is too tall or sticks out too far. This helps ensure the load can be handled safely and meets the required size.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-spec height/overhang remaining undetected during verification lets nonconforming loads pass, relevant to conformance; overhang/height issues cause handling and shipment problems.
rows.sat_rat: Automated profile/height scanners exist but are not universal, so undetected deviations remain a meaningful gap. Height and overhang conformance checks are typically manual spot checks that add some verification time. Detecting out-of-spec height reliably depends on inspector attention and knowing the spec, which is error-prone. Undetected out-of-spec loads cause downstream rejections and rework, a real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of replenished load bases that do not match required dimensions construction or load rating
rows.plain: Make sure replacement platforms are the right size, made properly, and strong enough to carry the required weight. This prevents loads from being unstable or damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Replenished load bases not matching required dimensions/construction/load rating undermines the foundation of stable stacking, relevant to load integrity; wrong pallets risk collapse.
rows.sat_rat: Pallet quality varies and dimension/rating mismatches are inconsistently screened at the machine. A wrong-spec base found late forces a stop and swap. Operators must recognize off-spec pallets, which relies on their attention. Wrong-rated bases risk load failure and costly rehandling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a load with an incorrect case position or orientation is accepted as conforming
rows.plain: Prevent approval of a load when its cases are in the wrong place or facing the wrong way. This helps ensure the load matches the required arrangement and can be handled safely.
rows.imp_rat: A load with incorrect case position/orientation accepted as conforming is a verification gate failure central to conformance; the escaped defect propagates to shipment.
rows.sat_rat: Automated verification is patchy, so incorrectly positioned loads are sometimes accepted as conforming. Any acceptance check adds little time since it is automated or visual at discharge. Judging conformance often still relies on operator spot-checks that demand attention. A nonconforming load accepted downstream causes costly returns and rehandling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases dimensionally incompatible with the specified load pattern during pallet building
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cases that do not fit the planned arrangement when goods are stacked on a pallet. Properly fitting cases help make the load stable and use space as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Cases dimensionally incompatible with the specified pattern block correct building, central to executing defined load patterns; consequences are misbuilds and jams.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming case dimensions vary and few lines gauge them before building, so incompatibility remains a real risk. Dimensional mismatches surface at build time and cause stoppages that consume time to clear. Recognizing a case that won't fit the pattern still relies on operator observation rather than automated gauging. Incompatible cases produce failed or reworked loads, a notable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of staged load bases with structural damage before palletizing
rows.plain: Make sure empty platforms waiting to be used are not cracked, bent, or otherwise damaged. Damaged platforms could fail or harm the goods during packing.
rows.imp_rat: Staged load bases with structural damage before palletizing compromises the stack foundation, relevant to stable-load integrity; damaged bases risk collapse downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Manual pallet inspection is inconsistent, so damaged bases still slip through at a real rate. A structurally damaged pallet found late stops the load, costing time. Judging pallet structural damage relies on operator visual inspection. Damaged pallets lead to collapsed loads, an expensive failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mixed products or lots remaining undetected during completed-load verification
rows.plain: Make sure mistakes such as mixed products or batches are unlikely to go unnoticed when a completed load is checked. This helps ensure the shipment contains exactly what it should.
rows.imp_rat: Mixed products/lots undetected during load verification lets segregation failures ship, relevant to correct composition; consequences include recall and compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Manual verification catches only some mixing, so undetected mixed loads remain a real residual risk. Detecting mixing at completed-load verification is a manual check that adds inspection time. Catching mixed lots depends on reading labels and operator attention rather than automation. Undetected mixing causes recalls or scrap, a high-cost failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in load pattern specifications about case orientation, layer sequence, pallet boundaries, or allowable variation
rows.plain: Make the instructions for arranging cases on a pallet clear about which way cases face, how layers are built, where the pallet ends, and what small differences are allowed. Clear instructions help workers pack every pallet correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in pattern specs about orientation, sequence, boundaries, or variation is a root-cause driver of misbuilds, central to correctly executing defined patterns; consequences are systematic errors.
rows.sat_rat: Load pattern specs are commonly incomplete or informal about orientation and allowable variation, leaving recurring interpretation errors. Producing unambiguous specs takes extra documentation time that is often skipped under schedule pressure. Writing precise orientation, sequence, and boundary specs demands disciplined technical authoring skill that varies widely. Ambiguous specs cause misbuilds and rework whose cost is frequently absorbed rather than prevented.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of recurrence of the same load instability after corrective closure
rows.plain: Prevent the same problem with an unstable load from happening again after the earlier problem was fixed and closed out. This avoids repeated failures and interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence of the same instability after closure indicates ineffective corrective action; relevant to sustained stable output but a reliability/process concern with moderate stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Corrective actions are often quick fixes that do not hold, so the same instability tends to reappear after closure. Recurrence forces the whole investigation cycle to repeat, extending overall resolution time significantly. Preventing recurrence requires root-cause discipline that most palletizing operations lack the dedicated expertise to sustain. Repeated instability of the same type multiplies rework and rejection costs that a good fix would have eliminated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of pallet-configuration deviations that operators fail to recognize or escalate
rows.plain: Operators should be able to notice when pallets are arranged incorrectly and report the problem. This helps prevent loading or handling mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Operators failing to recognize/escalate configuration deviations is a detection/human-factor gap relevant to catching pattern errors; consequences are moderate escaped defects.
rows.sat_rat: Because it hinges on human attention across shifts, recognition is inconsistent and deviations routinely slip past less-experienced staff. Escalation depends on an operator noticing and stopping, which can lag well behind the actual deviation. Recognizing and escalating configuration deviations rests almost entirely on operator judgement and experience with little decision support. Missed escalations let bad pallets accumulate, multiplying rework and scrap cost before anyone intervenes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cases dropped or subjected to damaging impacts during transfer and stacking
rows.plain: Reduce the number of packages that are dropped or hit hard while they are moved and stacked. This helps prevent damage to the products inside.
rows.imp_rat: Cases dropped or subjected to damaging impacts during transfer/stacking directly damages product during the core placement operation; frequent occurrence causes loss.
rows.sat_rat: Well-maintained grippers and conveyors handle cases consistently, but drops and hard impacts still occur at transfer points and during jams. Automated transfer runs at set speed without time cost to gentleness. Machine handling removes most manual lifting skill, though gripper tuning needs technician expertise. Dropped cases mean lost product plus cleanup and line stoppage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a specified pattern violates lot, product, or destination segregation requirements
rows.plain: The planned arrangement should not mix products, batches, or destinations in a way that breaks separation rules. This helps ensure items go to the right place without being mixed up.
rows.imp_rat: A specified pattern violating lot/product/destination segregation is a compliance design failure relevant to correct load composition; consequences include recall and regulatory exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation requirements are inconsistently encoded into pattern specs, leaving a recurring risk of non-compliant mixed loads. Segregation checks add time when rules must be looked up per lot or destination. Knowing all lot, product, and destination segregation rules requires distributed regulatory and customer knowledge. Segregation violations cause costly returns and compliance penalties only partly prevented today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which worker injury severity from stabilizing unstable or damaged pallet loads is underestimated
rows.plain: Make sure the possible seriousness of injuries is not underestimated when workers steady unsafe or damaged loads on pallets. This helps the buyer focus safety efforts where they are most needed.
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating injury severity when stabilizing unstable loads is a risk-assessment gap; relevant to safety planning but indirect to core stacking, though injury consequences are high.
rows.sat_rat: Severity is routinely underestimated because consequence rating remains subjective and inconsistent. Severity estimation is a bounded analysis task without major time pressure. Correctly gauging injury severity from stabilizing unstable loads demands experienced risk-assessment judgement that is scarce. The estimation costs little, though underestimation leads to costly injuries.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cases shifting or falling beyond the pallet footprint during removal
rows.plain: Prevent packages from moving or falling outside the pallet’s base while the finished load is removed. This helps protect the packages and people nearby.
rows.imp_rat: Cases shifting/falling beyond the pallet footprint during removal is a stability failure at handoff, relevant to stable-load delivery; falling cases cause damage and injury.
rows.sat_rat: Load squaring keeps cases within footprint fairly well, though marginal loads still shift during removal. A shifting load during removal can stall discharge. Squaring and stabilizing devices reduce the manual care needed at removal. Fallen cases beyond the footprint are scrap and cleanup cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pallets built to a superseded load pattern after a specification change
rows.plain: Prevent pallets from being built using an old loading arrangement after the requirements have changed. This helps ensure the load matches the current instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Pallets built to a superseded pattern after a spec change produces systematically wrong loads, relevant to conformance; consequences are batch-scale rejection but usually detectable.
rows.sat_rat: Version control on load-pattern recipes is uneven, so stale patterns persist on some lines after a spec change until noticed. Pushing a new pattern recipe to the machine is quick once triggered, but the lag between spec approval and line update is often long. Change control relies on someone correctly loading and verifying the new recipe, a judgement-dependent handoff. Pallets built to a superseded pattern are frequently reworked or scrapped, a direct avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of case seams opening during placement or stacking
rows.plain: Reduce the share of package seams that come open while packages are being placed or stacked. This helps keep the contents protected during handling.
rows.imp_rat: Case seams opening during placement/stacking damages product integrity in the core operation; consequences are spillage, contamination, and loss, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Seam failures under stacking pressure occur intermittently with weak sealing, giving fair consistency. Opened seams during stacking can stop the line for cleanup, costing time. Seam integrity depends on upstream sealing, not palletizer operator skill. Opened cases spill product and are lost, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of cases damaged during palletizing
rows.plain: Reduce the share of packages that are damaged while being arranged on a pallet. This helps more products reach the next step in good condition.
rows.imp_rat: Cases damaged during palletizing is a broad product-integrity outcome directly tied to the core operation; frequent damage causes significant product loss.
rows.sat_rat: Modern palletizers with proper grip and pattern control keep damage rates moderate but not negligible. Damage-related jams and clean-up occasionally interrupt the line but are usually brief. Reducing damage during palletizing depends on correct machine setup and handling technique that experienced operators manage reasonably well. Some case damage during palletizing is an accepted low-level cost, mitigated but not eliminated by tuned equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a dense pattern cannot be executed within palletizer case-handling and placement constraints
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a tightly packed arrangement cannot be handled or placed by the pallet-moving equipment. The load must be dense without preventing the equipment from moving cases safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: A dense pattern unexecutable within palletizer constraints blocks producing the intended load, relevant to pattern execution capability; the specific dense load cannot be built.
rows.sat_rat: Density and machine executability often conflict, so dense specs somewhat frequently prove unrunnable on the actual palletizer. Verifying a dense pattern against machine limits is quick when capability data is on hand. Reconciling maximum density with palletizer grip and placement limits requires specialized programming skill. Dense patterns that cannot run force fallback to lower-density loads, eroding shipping efficiency.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of worker exposure to excessive lifting, reaching, twisting, or force under specified handling arrangements
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that workers must lift, reach, twist, or use too much force while handling materials. Safer handling protects them from strain and injury.
rows.imp_rat: Worker exposure to excessive lifting/twisting is an ergonomic concern relevant to how loads are handled, but the automated stacking job can succeed even with some manual exposure; injuries are serious but typically cumulative rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Established ergonomic tools like NIOSH lifting equations reduce but do not consistently catch every hazardous posture under real load patterns. Ergonomic assessments of lifting and reaching exist but are done periodically rather than continuously, so exposure between reviews goes unmeasured. Recognizing excessive lifting, twisting, and force loads requires trained ergonomists whose judgement varies widely between assessors. Standard checklists and lift calculators are inexpensive, but corrective redesigns of handling arrangements carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of worker interaction points with unresolved crush, pinch, or struck-by hazards
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times workers must interact with equipment while risks of crushing, pinching, or being hit have not been resolved. This helps prevent worker injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Worker interaction points with unresolved hazards is a safety design issue relevant to safe operation but peripheral to the automated stacking CFJ; injury stakes are notable.
rows.sat_rat: Standard hazard inventories catch many points but unresolved ones consistently slip through. Counting and closing interaction points is a periodic exercise, leaving gaps between reviews. Identifying every unresolved interaction hazard requires systematic expertise often stretched thin. Eliminating interaction points via automation or guarding is capital intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cases from the wrong product or lot entering the palletizing queue
rows.plain: Keep cases containing the wrong product or production lot out of the line that prepares them for pallet loading. This helps prevent shipping and product-identification errors.
rows.imp_rat: Cases from the wrong product/lot entering the palletizing queue is an upstream segregation error feeding mix defects, relevant to correct composition; consequences include recall exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and scanning reduce but don't eliminate wrong-lot entry. A wrong-lot case in the queue may stop building until resolved, costing time. Preventing wrong-lot entry relies on upstream routing and scanning, not operator skill. Wrong-lot cases cause mixed loads and scrap, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cased-good configurations without an applicable load pattern specification
rows.plain: Make sure every packaged-product arrangement has a clear plan for how the load should be placed. This helps ensure each load is handled and processed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Configurations without an applicable load pattern spec means no valid basis to stack them, central to the CFJ; without a spec the job cannot be done correctly for those goods.
rows.sat_rat: Pattern libraries cover most SKUs but new or edge configurations regularly lack an applicable specification. Coverage gaps surface late, requiring reactive pattern creation that delays shipments. Generating a specification for an uncovered configuration needs the same specifier expertise, a recurring bottleneck. Missing specs cause ad hoc manual stacking that raises labor and damage cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that case damage or leakage hazards created by the load pattern remain unidentified
rows.plain: Make sure the way cases are loaded does not hide damage or create leaks. This helps ensure the load can be handled and transported safely.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified damage/leakage hazards from the load pattern is a detection gap relevant to product integrity; leakage can cause contamination and downstream damage.
rows.sat_rat: Load-pattern-induced damage is frequently unidentified until it occurs, indicating weak preventive practice. Damage and leakage hazards are usually caught late, at inspection or downstream, rather than at pattern design. Predicting which patterns cause case damage requires product-specific packaging knowledge. Leakage and damage generate product loss and cleanup cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases crushed or buckled under stacking pressure during palletizing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping cases that are crushed or bent when cases are stacked on pallets. This protects the products inside.
rows.imp_rat: Cases crushed/buckled under stacking pressure is a product-integrity failure of the stacking arrangement, central to producing shippable loads; damage causes loss.
rows.sat_rat: Correct patterns limit crushing, but weak cartons and humidity still cause sporadic buckling, leaving moderate residual failure. Crushing occurs during stacking and any resulting stoppage to clear damaged cases costs time. Preventing crush relies on correct pattern setup rather than in-the-moment operator skill. Crushed and buckled cases are direct product loss and rework, a meaningful recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a corrective change introduces case damage, load overhang, obstructed identification, or another rejection condition
rows.plain: Make sure a fix does not damage the case, leave the load sticking out, hide required labels, or create another reason for rejection. This helps the corrected load pass inspection.
rows.imp_rat: A corrective change introducing new rejection conditions undermines recovery effectiveness, relevant to sustained conformance; the reintroduced defect causes further rejection.
rows.sat_rat: Corrective tweaks are usually validated narrowly, so introducing a new defect while fixing another is a recurring surprise. Verifying a change does not create a new rejection condition adds validation time that is often skipped. Anticipating side effects like overhang or obscured labels requires broad knowledge of all rejection criteria at once. A change that introduces damage or overhang creates fresh scrap and reject costs, undoing the intended savings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of case overhang beyond the defined pallet footprint
rows.plain: Keep each box within the usable area of the pallet and prevent it from sticking out. This makes loads safer and allows the available space to be used well.
rows.imp_rat: Case overhang beyond the defined pallet footprint degrades stability and downstream fit, central to correct arrangement; overhang causes handling damage and rejection.
rows.sat_rat: Overhang is an explicit, easily computed constraint that most pattern tools enforce reliably. Overhang is quickly checked geometrically against the pallet footprint. Detecting overhang requires little specialized skill once footprint is defined. Overhang causes damage and rejections but the preventive check is cheap and routine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed pallets requiring rework because of unstable case arrangement
rows.plain: Fewer finished pallets should need fixing because the boxes are stacked unstably. This helps avoid extra work and keeps products from falling or being damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Rework due to unstable arrangement is a productivity/quality issue tied to correct stacking, but it is caught and corrected internally, limiting severity to cost and delay.
rows.sat_rat: Unstable case arrangements recur regularly because pattern control and case quality vary, so rework is a routine cost the role absorbs. Reworking an unstable pallet consumes real line and labor time that is not planned for in throughput targets. Deciding whether a formed pallet needs rebuilding relies on inconsistent operator eyeballing rather than a defined check. Rework wastes wrap, labor and sometimes damaged cases, and the status quo tolerates a steady baseline of it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the same palletizing disruption recurs after output is restored
rows.plain: The same problem with stacking products on pallets should happen less often after production has restarted. This helps keep work running smoothly instead of repeatedly stopping.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring palletizing disruption after output restoration signals ineffective fixes, relevant to reliable sustained operation; consequences are repeated downtime, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Fixes are typically reactive quick restarts, so the same disruption recurs frequently rather than being permanently eliminated. Recurring disruptions repeatedly consume restoration time because root causes aren't fixed. Preventing recurrence requires diagnostic skill that is often unavailable on the floor. Each recurrence adds downtime and rework cost that compounds over shifts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that palletizing is declared recovered while disruption symptoms remain
rows.plain: The stacking operation should be unlikely to be called recovered while signs of the problem are still present. This helps prevent work from being treated as fixed before it is actually stable.
rows.imp_rat: Declaring palletizing recovered while symptoms persist lets latent problems continue, relevant to genuine restoration; consequences are recurring disruption and escaped defects.
rows.sat_rat: Without confirmation criteria, palletizing is often declared recovered while symptoms persist, making false recovery a routine occurrence. Declaring recovery early to resume output is common, trading verification time for uptime. Judging true recovery versus lingering symptoms depends on experienced eyes not standard checks. Premature recovery calls cause repeat stoppages and more scrap, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between incoming case orientation and the orientation required by the stacking pattern
rows.plain: Make sure incoming boxes face the right direction for the planned stack. This makes placing them safely and efficiently easier.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between incoming and required case orientation directly threatens pattern correctness and interlock stability, central to the CFJ; consequences are misbuilds and instability.
rows.sat_rat: Case turners driven by the pattern recipe reliably deliver correct orientation, though sensor or slip errors occasionally slip through. Turn devices and orientation stations reorient cases in stride without slowing the line. Automated turners set orientation per recipe so operators seldom manually align cases. Wrong-orientation cases can force teardown, but automated turning keeps such waste infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a specified unit load exceeds an allowable dimensional or weight limit
rows.plain: Make sure the planned load does not exceed the allowed size or weight. This helps the load fit safely and prevents handling or transport problems.
rows.imp_rat: A specified unit load exceeding dimensional/weight limits produces unshippable loads and safety hazards, relevant to shippability; overweight loads risk equipment and transport failure.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional and weight limits are hard numbers routinely enforced by software, so violations are among the least frequent failures. Height and weight limit checks are near-instant arithmetic in any pattern tool. Applying documented dimensional and weight limits requires little specialized skill. Over-limit loads risk fines and rejections, but the checks preventing them are cheap and routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of crushing or deformation in lower cases under the specified stacking arrangement
rows.plain: Prevent the boxes at the bottom of a stack from being crushed or misshapen. This protects the products and keeps the stack stable.
rows.imp_rat: Crushing/deformation of lower cases under stacking arrangement compromises both product integrity and load stability, central to correct pattern design; consequences are product damage.
rows.sat_rat: Compression-strength rules and stacking limits are reasonably established, but variable case strength and humidity still produce crush failures. Compression estimates are available in software fairly quickly for typical cases. Interpreting stacking-strength versus load height still requires packaging engineering judgement not always on hand. Crushed lower cases mean scrapped product and claims that current practice only partially prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unsafe worker exposure during palletizing remains undetected until the next compliance review
rows.plain: Make sure unsafe worker exposure during palletizing is noticed promptly, rather than only during a later compliance check. This helps protect workers from avoidable harm.
rows.imp_rat: Unsafe worker exposure undetected until the next compliance review is a delayed-detection safety gap, peripheral to automated stacking; prolonged unaddressed exposure raises injury risk.
rows.sat_rat: Detection is unreliable between reviews, so latent exposure persists undetected. Unsafe exposure often stays hidden until the next scheduled compliance review, creating long detection delays. Detecting exposure early requires continuous monitoring skill rarely deployed between reviews. Continuous monitoring would cost more than the periodic reviews used today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of case overhang beyond the specified pallet footprint
rows.plain: Keep cases from sticking out beyond the required edges of the pallet. This helps make the load stable and easier to handle safely.
rows.imp_rat: Case overhang beyond the pallet footprint degrades stability and downstream fit, central to correct arrangement; overhang causes handling damage and rejection.
rows.sat_rat: When the recipe matches the case and pallet, overhang is tightly and consistently controlled, though case-size variation and slippage still cause occasional excess. Overhang is set by the pattern geometry at run time with no time penalty. Correct footprint depends on accurate recipe setup rather than ongoing operator skill. Overhanging cases get crushed or snagged, causing product loss and handling problems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of staged cases with damage or compromised closures before palletizing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of staged cases that are damaged or have broken or weakened closures before they are stacked on pallets. This helps protect the goods during handling and shipment.
rows.imp_rat: Staged cases with damage/compromised closures before palletizing propagates defects into loads, relevant to product integrity; upstream-driven, causing loss and contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-stage inspection is partial, so damaged cases still enter palletizing intermittently. Detecting damaged staged cases before building adds some inspection time. Spotting compromised closures depends on operator attention rather than automation. Damaged cases entering the build cause rework and loss, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the actual cause of load instability or shipment rejection is misidentified
rows.plain: Correctly identify what really caused a load to become unstable or a shipment to be rejected. This helps the team fix the real problem instead of the wrong one.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the cause of instability/rejection prolongs and misdirects corrective action; relevant to sustained stable output but a diagnostic concern with indirect stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Cause identification is often guesswork based on the most visible symptom, so misdiagnosis is common. Misidentified causes send teams chasing wrong fixes, dragging out resolution over multiple cycles. Correctly diagnosing why a load failed demands analytical expertise across packaging, pattern and handling that is rarely resident on the floor. Wrong-cause fixes waste money on changes that do not solve the problem while the real defect persists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases oriented incorrectly within the specified load pattern during pallet building
rows.plain: Reduce the share of boxes placed in the wrong direction or position within the required pallet arrangement. This helps keep the pallet stable and ready for storage or shipping.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect case orientation directly violates the defined load pattern central to the CFJ; misorientation weakens stability and interlock, causing moderate rejection/damage consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical orientation devices and recipe control deliver consistent case orientation, so deviations are uncommon in well-maintained lines. Orientation is set by tooling and infeed devices without adding cycle time, and turn devices handle it inline. Case-turning stations and programmed grippers remove most operator judgement about orientation within the pattern. Occasional mis-orientation produces unstable or non-conforming loads that must be rebuilt, a modest recurring waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between each case feed position and its specified layer position
rows.plain: Place each case as close as possible to the spot assigned to it in the layer. This keeps the goods arranged correctly for the next step.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between case feed position and specified layer position is core to correct layer formation; small deviations are usually correctable, so consequences are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Programmed layer patterns and mechanical squaring make each feed position consistently match its specified layer slot. Layer building proceeds at machine pace with programmed positions, adding no delay when set correctly. Recipe-driven layer maps mean operators rarely judge individual layer positions by hand. Layer position errors mostly waste minor rework rather than product, keeping cost impact modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between incoming case placement and the required load-forming position
rows.plain: Place each incoming case in the exact position needed to build the finished load. This helps the cases form a stable and correct arrangement.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between incoming case placement and required position directly undermines accurate load forming; errors are common and cause misalignment, though individually recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Guide rails, stops and servo-positioned layer tables make correct placement highly repeatable, so deviation is well controlled on standard formats. Modern palletizer infeed guides and layer-forming tables position cases quickly without adding cycle time. Mechanical guides and programmed layer forming remove most operator finesse from placing cases in the load-forming position. Occasional misplacements cause rework and product damage, but these are a minor cost fraction on tuned lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a positioned case tips or shifts before arrangement into the load
rows.plain: Keep a positioned case from tipping or moving before it is arranged in the load. This helps prevent damage and keeps the load stable.
rows.imp_rat: A positioned case tipping/shifting before arrangement is an in-process placement-stability failure central to accurate stacking; consequences are misplacement and cascade instability.
rows.sat_rat: Layer supports keep positioned cases stable, though light or tall cases still tip occasionally. A tipped case mid-arrangement stops the cycle to recover. Supports and holders reduce the manual steadying operators must do. Tipped cases occasionally scrap product but usually just delay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of case deformation caused by unsupported overhang beyond the pallet footprint
rows.plain: Prevent cases from bending or being crushed when part of them extends beyond the pallet without support. This helps keep the cases intact during stacking and movement.
rows.imp_rat: Case deformation caused by unsupported overhang combines a stability defect with product damage, central to correct footprint arrangement; consequences are loss and instability.
rows.sat_rat: Proper pattern selection controls overhang, but mismatched case dimensions still cause deformation. Overhang issues surface at inspection rather than causing immediate delay. Correct pattern programming avoids overhang without operator intervention. Overhang deforms cases and risks load rejection, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cases punctured, torn, or abraded during palletizing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping boxes that are pierced, ripped, or scraped while being stacked on pallets. This keeps the products protected during handling and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Cases punctured/torn/abraded during palletizing damages product integrity during the core operation; consequences are contamination and rejection, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Smooth gripper and conveyor surfaces keep abrasion low, but worn guides, sharp edges, and jams still cause intermittent puncturing. Handling speed is unrelated to abrasion, so no time impact. Puncture/tear avoidance depends on equipment condition and setup more than operator skill. Torn or punctured cases are scrapped and may breach primary containers, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified load and staging arrangements obstruct worker travel or emergency egress
rows.plain: Choose load and staging arrangements that do not block workers’ paths or emergency exits. This helps people move around safely and leave quickly if needed.
rows.imp_rat: Load/staging arrangements obstructing worker travel or emergency egress is a serious safety hazard, but peripheral to the stacking function itself; blocked egress is dangerous.
rows.sat_rat: Fire and egress codes are mature and enforced, so obstruction of egress is reasonably well controlled. Egress and travel path checks are part of standard facility layout review done infrequently. Facility safety officers apply established egress codes, so skill demands are moderate and well supported. Layout compliance is low cost once designed into staging.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an operator uses superseded palletizing requirements after a standard change
rows.plain: Prevent workers from following old pallet-stacking instructions after the standard has changed. Using the current instructions helps ensure loads are stacked correctly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: An operator using superseded palletizing requirements produces wrong loads, relevant to conformance; a human/process control issue with batch-scale but detectable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on training and postings rather than enforced current-version gating makes stale-requirement use a recurring risk across shifts. Communicating a standard change to every shift takes time, leaving a window where old requirements are still followed. Whether an operator applies current requirements depends on their awareness and diligence rather than a system-enforced lockout. Using superseded requirements produces nonconforming pallets that must be reworked, wasting labor and material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in applying approved load requirements across product configurations and shifts
rows.plain: Apply the approved requirements for arranging and securing loads the same way for every product setup and work shift. This helps prevent load problems caused by inconsistent practices.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in applying approved load requirements across configs/shifts produces inconsistent conformance, relevant to reliable pattern execution; consequences are sporadic misbuilds.
rows.sat_rat: Load requirements are applied inconsistently across product configurations and shifts because they rely on manual interpretation. Inconsistent application across shifts causes rework loops that consume time unpredictably. Uniform application depends on every operator interpreting load requirements the same way, which varies widely by skill and shift. Variation drives sporadic waste and rework whose cost is hard to control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cases punctured or torn by contact surfaces during palletizing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of shipping boxes that are pierced or ripped when they touch surfaces while being stacked on pallets. This helps keep the products protected and the boxes safe to handle.
rows.imp_rat: Cases punctured/torn by contact surfaces during palletizing damages product in the core operation; consequences are contamination and loss, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Well-guarded contact surfaces make punctures uncommon on maintained equipment. Punctures usually don't stop the line immediately, so time impact is small. Avoiding puncture depends on equipment guarding rather than operator skill. Torn cases mean damaged product, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases crushed or permanently deformed during palletizing
rows.plain: Reduce the share of shipping boxes that are crushed or permanently misshapen while being stacked on pallets. This helps protect the products and keep the pallet stable.
rows.imp_rat: Cases crushed or permanently deformed during palletizing is a direct product-integrity failure of the core operation; damage causes product loss and customer rejection.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered stack heights and patterns keep crushing low in normal running, though overhang, misalignment, and unstable stacks still deform some cases. Compression is a function of stacking, adding no cycle time. Crush avoidance is designed into layer limits and patterns rather than operator skill. Crushed cases are scrapped, a direct product loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of damaged or deformed cases that stop placement in the specified pattern
rows.plain: Reduce the number of damaged or misshapen boxes that prevent workers or equipment from placing them in the required arrangement. This helps packing continue without interruption.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged/deformed cases that stop placement in the specified pattern block correct build and halt operation, relevant to reliable pattern execution; consequences are stoppage and misbuild.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming damage varies and defect rejection is imperfect, so such stops recur. A damaged case that stops placement interrupts flow and costs time to clear. Handling damaged cases still needs operator intervention rather than automated recovery. The damaged case is lost and the stoppage wastes capacity, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pallets containing cases from different batches during palletizing
rows.plain: Keep cases from different production groups on separate pallets. This makes it easier to track where each case came from.
rows.imp_rat: Pallets containing cases from different batches is a batch-segregation failure relevant to traceable load composition; consequences include recall scope and compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Formal batch-clearance practice keeps mixing rare, giving reasonable consistency. Batch changeover procedures add some clearance time but not to individual builds. Standard changeover checklists reduce dependence on operator memory to prevent batch mixing. Batch mixing can invalidate loads and force rework, a significant cost when missed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a pallet load contains cases from an unintended product or lot
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a pallet contains cases from the wrong product or production group. This helps ensure the shipment contains exactly what was intended.
rows.imp_rat: A pallet containing cases from an unintended product/lot is a traceability/mix error relevant to correct load composition; consequences include recall and compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated line clearance and scanning make wrong-product mixing rare where enforced, though manual overrides and shared lines leave residual risk. Scan-based verification adds only seconds when in place. Barcode/lot scanning reduces dependence on operators to visually distinguish products. A mixed-lot pallet in pharma can force costly quarantine or full-batch rejection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pallets containing cases from different products or lots during pallet building
rows.plain: Keep pallets from containing cases from different products or production groups when the pallet is built. This makes the contents easier to identify and prevents mix-ups.
rows.imp_rat: Pallets containing cases from different products/lots during building is a segregation/traceability failure relevant to correct load composition; consequences include recall and compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated lanes and clearance procedures make mixing uncommon, so this is fairly well served. Segregation is enforced by line clearance and scheduling without adding per-load time. Line-clearance procedures reduce reliance on operator vigilance to keep products separate. A mixed pallet can force scrap or full re-inspection, an expensive failure when it occurs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases crushed torn or opened while being fed into a layer
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cases that are crushed, torn, or opened while being placed into a layer of the load. This helps keep the goods intact during loading.
rows.imp_rat: Cases crushed/torn/opened while fed into a layer damages product during the core feeding operation; consequences are loss and contamination, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Feed mechanisms are gentle enough that tearing is uncommon but not eliminated. Torn cases fed into a layer may force a stop to remove them. Gentle feed mechanisms lessen the manual care needed to avoid tearing. Cases torn during feeding become scrap and add cleanup cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cases crushed by compressive force during load formation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cases damaged by being squeezed too hard while a load is being formed. This protects the products and packaging from damage.
rows.imp_rat: Cases crushed by compressive force during load formation is a stacking-arrangement damage failure central to product integrity; consequences are loss and instability.
rows.sat_rat: Compression is generally controlled, though heavy layers still crush weaker cases at times. Crush damage seldom halts production immediately. Programmed compression limits reduce dependence on operator judgement. Crushed cases are direct scrap, a persistent low-level cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of handling failures caused by out-of-specification incoming cases
rows.plain: Reduce handling problems caused by incoming cases that do not meet the required condition or size. This helps goods move through the process without delays or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Handling failures caused by out-of-spec incoming cases disrupts stacking, relevant to reliable operation; upstream-driven consequences are jams and misbuilds, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming case dimensional and quality variation is a persistent driver of handling failures with weak upstream control. Out-of-spec cases cause jams and stoppages that eat into run time before anyone traces the source. Detecting incoming case defects before they cause handling failures relies on operator vigilance rather than automated screening. Bad incoming cases cause damage and downtime whose cost is often borne downstream at palletizing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to reproduce intermittent load instability under representative storage and transport conditions
rows.plain: Make it possible to reproduce occasional load problems under storage and transport conditions that reflect real use. This helps people find out why the load becomes unstable and how to fix it.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to reproduce intermittent instability hampers root-cause resolution, relevant to eliminating recurring instability; a diagnostic capability gap with indirect, moderate stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent instability is notoriously hard to reproduce with typical shop-floor means, so causes stay unconfirmed. Chasing an intermittent fault that won't reproduce stretches investigations out indefinitely. Recreating representative storage and transport stresses demands specialized test capability rarely available in-house. Failure to reproduce leads to repeated field failures whose cumulative cost far exceeds a proper test.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the stacking pattern used for feeding differs from the production order
rows.plain: Make sure the arrangement used to feed products into the machine matches the planned production order. This helps the machine handle the products correctly.
rows.imp_rat: The feeding stacking pattern differing from the production order produces systematically wrong loads, central to building the correct pattern; consequences are batch rejection.
rows.sat_rat: When the MES pushes the pattern automatically it is reliable, but manual selection leaves room for mismatch. Selecting the correct pattern is a quick recipe recall with no meaningful delay. Order-linked recipe selection reduces operator judgement in choosing the pattern. A wrong pattern produces a scrapped or reworked load, a notable but rare cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cases dented or ruptured by collisions during load formation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cases that are dented or torn open when they bump into one another while a load is being built. This helps protect the products and packaging.
rows.imp_rat: Cases dented/ruptured by collisions during load formation damages product in the core operation; consequences are product loss and contamination, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Soft-stop and guided motion reduce collision damage, but tight patterns still cause occasional impacts. Collision damage rarely stops the line but may require removing the damaged case. Controlled motion profiles minimize the need for operators to prevent collisions. Dented or ruptured cases are scrap, adding measurable product cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case jams at the palletizer infeed
rows.plain: Reduce how often cases get stuck where they enter the pallet-building machine. This helps keep the loading process moving steadily.
rows.imp_rat: Case jams at the palletizer infeed stop the core feeding process, highly relevant to reliable throughput; consequences are line stoppage and misfeeds, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Infeed jamming is one of the most persistent chronic failure modes on palletizers. Infeed jams halt flow and require stopping to clear, costing throughput. Freeing infeed jams is a frequent manual task needing operator attention. Repeated jams cause case damage and downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among stability criteria applicable to the completed load
rows.plain: Reduce conflicts between the different requirements for keeping the completed load stable. This helps ensure the load will not shift, fall, or become damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among stability criteria for the completed load creates inconsistent acceptance decisions, relevant to reliable stability assurance; consequences are ambiguous pass/fail with moderate impact.
rows.sat_rat: Absent harmonized criteria, decisions vary between operators, leaving consistency weak. Resolving conflicting stability criteria stalls decisions and adds time. Reconciling competing stability rules demands experienced judgement that few standardized aids support. Conflicts lead to inconsistent accept/reject decisions and rework, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pallet positions with hazardous reaches, lifts, or postures overlooked during the assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the safety review does not miss any pallet positions that require dangerous reaching, lifting, or body positions. This helps reduce the risk of worker injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked hazardous reaches/lifts/postures in the assessment is an ergonomic assessment gap, peripheral to automated stacking; unaddressed exposure causes cumulative injury.
rows.sat_rat: Position-by-position hazards are frequently overlooked because coverage depends on assessor diligence. Assessing every pallet position takes time but is a scheduled activity. Spotting hazardous reaches and postures at each position requires attentive ergonomic expertise that is easily overtaxed. The assessment is inexpensive relative to injury costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed pallets containing an incorrect layer case count or sequence
rows.plain: Make sure every finished pallet has the correct number and order of cases in each layer. This helps keep the load stable and matches the approved packing arrangement.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect layer case count or sequence violates the defined load pattern and can affect stability and inventory accuracy; consequences are moderate and usually detectable.
rows.sat_rat: PLC-driven row and layer sequencing is generally dependable, but sensor faults, doubles, or misfeeds still let incorrect counts through undetected on some pallets.
