Layer-count logic and case counters run at line speed, adding no delay. Automated counting and indexing reduce reliance on operator vigilance for per-layer counts. Miscounted layers cause partial-pallet rework, a limited but recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case-feed jams during layer formation
rows.plain: Reduce how often cases get stuck while being fed into layers. This helps packing continue smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Case-feed jams during layer formation halt the core stacking process, highly relevant to reliable pattern building; consequences are stoppage and potential damage, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Jams during layer formation remain a common failure mode despite guide tuning. Layer-formation jams stop the machine and consume clearing time each occurrence. Jam clearing during layer forming often requires practiced manual intervention. Jams damage cases and lose production time, driving recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incoming cases too damaged or weak for load formation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of incoming cases that are too damaged or weak to be arranged into a stable load. This helps prevent packing problems and product damage.
rows.imp_rat: Incoming cases too damaged/weak for load formation cannot support the pattern, relevant to buildable stable loads; upstream-driven, causing misbuilds and instability.
rows.sat_rat: There is little systematic rejection of weak cases before they reach the palletizer. Weak cases usually fail during forming, causing an interrupting jam. Judging case strength is manual and inconsistent. Too-weak cases collapse and generate scrap and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assessing a completed load against an incorrect or superseded stacking specification
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of checking a finished load against an old or wrong stacking guide. This helps ensure the load is judged using the correct arrangement.
rows.imp_rat: Assessing a load against an incorrect/superseded stacking spec invalidates verification, relevant to conformance; wrong-baseline acceptance lets nonconforming loads pass.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems mostly ensure current specs, but manual paper systems still allow superseded use. Verifying against the current spec is quick when spec control is digital. Retrieving the right spec version depends on document control rather than operator skill. Using a superseded spec causes wrong builds and rework, a real cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of incoming cases with deformation or closure damage at the load-forming point
rows.plain: Reduce the number of incoming cases that are bent or have damaged closures when they reach the point where they are arranged for loading. This matters because damaged cases can cause handling and loading problems.
rows.imp_rat: Incoming cases with deformation/closure damage at load forming propagates defects and weakens the load, relevant to product and stability integrity; upstream-driven, moderate impact.
rows.sat_rat: Upstream case-damage screening is limited, so deformed cases often arrive at load forming. Spotting damaged cases inline adds little time if automated. Identifying deformed or open cases largely relies on operator observation. Damaged incoming cases become scrap and cause downstream instability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of corrective changes approved without representative coverage of case configurations, load patterns, storage, and transport conditions
rows.plain: Make sure approved changes are tested across the different case types, load arrangements, storage conditions, and transport conditions they are meant to cover. This matters because a change that works in one situation may fail in another.
rows.imp_rat: Corrective changes approved without representative coverage risks ineffective fixes, relevant to durable recovery; a validation-process concern with indirect, moderate stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Corrective changes are typically approved on limited samples, so many miss configurations that later fail. Skipping representative coverage seems faster upfront but forces re-work when the fix fails in untested conditions. Designing a change that covers all case configs, patterns and transport conditions requires test-design expertise seldom available. Approving under-tested changes wastes money on rollouts that fail in real storage and transport.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of manual force required to handle cased goods during foreseeable palletizer interventions
rows.plain: Reduce the physical effort needed to handle boxed goods when workers must step in during palletizing. This matters because less force makes the intervention safer and easier for workers.
rows.imp_rat: Manual force required during palletizer interventions is an ergonomic concern relevant only to exception handling, peripheral to automated stacking; injury stakes are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Where assist equipment exists, force is reliably reduced, but coverage is uneven. Reducing manual force is a design consideration that does not primarily affect assessment timing. Assist devices reduce reliance on operator strength once specified, lowering skill demands. Lift assists and conveyors reduce force but require investment, making cost the primary lever here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cases enter load forming before the format transition is complete
rows.plain: Make sure no cases move into the stacking process until the change to the new package size or setup is finished. This prevents the wrong cases from being handled together.
rows.imp_rat: Cases entering load forming before format transition completes causes mixed/wrong-pattern builds, relevant to correct changeover; consequences are misbuilds needing rework.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks help but incomplete transition sensing means early case entry still occasionally happens. Format transitions impose planned holds, and premature entry can cause added stoppage time. Transition interlocks reduce dependence on operators timing the changeover manually. Cases entering mid-transition cause jams and scrap that add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unusable pallets reaching the loading position during production runs
rows.plain: Prevent damaged or unusable pallets from reaching the loading position during production. This helps keep materials moving without interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Unusable pallets reaching the loading position disrupts the build and can compromise the base, relevant to reliable stacking; consequences are stoppage and unstable bases.
rows.sat_rat: Pallet quality varies and inspection is inconsistent, so unusable pallets still reach the position. An unusable pallet reaching the load position stops the cycle for replacement, costing time. Detecting bad pallets often relies on operator spotting rather than automated inspection. Bad pallets cause failed loads and rework, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration from disruption onset until stable conforming palletizing output is restored
rows.plain: Restore steady production of acceptable pallet loads as quickly as possible after a disruption begins. This reduces downtime and limits the effect on production.
rows.imp_rat: Duration from disruption onset to restored conforming output is a downtime/recovery metric, relevant to sustained throughput; consequences are lost production time, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery duration is highly variable, with no standard rapid-restore procedure guaranteeing stable conforming output quickly. Restoration time depends on operator troubleshooting and technician availability, so downtime is often prolonged. Speed of recovery is bottlenecked by scarce skilled maintenance and diagnosis. Extended disruptions accrue downtime and idle-labor costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a completed load with an incorrect case count during conformance verification
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of approving a finished load that contains the wrong number of cases. This helps ensure the shipment or batch matches what was required.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a completed load with incorrect case count during verification is a conformance gate failure, relevant to correct load composition; escaped count errors affect inventory and orders.
rows.sat_rat: Counters and programmed layer counts make count accuracy fairly reliable, though manual reconciliation still errs occasionally. Case-count verification is typically quick when automated counters feed the record. Automatic case counters reduce reliance on manual counting skill. Accepting a wrong count causes shipping shortages or overages, a real but contained cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cases dropped during transfer onto pallets
rows.plain: Prevent cases from falling while they are being moved onto pallets. This protects the cases from damage.
rows.imp_rat: Cases dropped during transfer onto pallets damages product in the core placement step; consequences are product loss and jams, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Well-maintained end effectors drop cases rarely, but grip failures on some case types still occur. A dropped case stops the line and consumes cleanup time. Automated grippers and clamps reduce reliance on manual handling to prevent drops. Dropped cases are direct product loss, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of damaging collisions between cases and palletizing surfaces
rows.plain: Prevent cases from hitting surfaces hard enough to cause damage while they are being placed. This keeps the cases usable.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging collisions between cases and palletizing surfaces damages product during the core operation; consequences are loss and contamination, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Programmed motion paths make damaging collisions infrequent when equipment is calibrated. Damaging collisions can halt the line and add recovery time. Collision avoidance is designed into machine motion, reducing operator dependence. Collisions damage cases and sometimes equipment, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases staged in the wrong orientation or sequence for the specified load pattern
rows.plain: Set out cases in the correct direction and order for the planned load. This helps the final stack be built correctly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Cases staged in wrong orientation/sequence feeds errors into pattern building, central to correct arrangement; usually caught before completion, so moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical orienters and conveyor logic keep staging orientation fairly consistent when maintained. Staging orientation is handled inline by orienting devices without materially slowing the line. Upstream orienting equipment removes most operator judgement over staging sequence and orientation. Wrong staged orientation causes downstream re-handling, a modest but recurring waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases fed in an orientation that differs from the defined stacking pattern
rows.plain: Place fewer cases in a direction that does not match the planned stacking arrangement. This helps the load stay stable and fit together properly.
rows.imp_rat: Cases fed in wrong orientation vs the defined pattern directly propagates orientation defects into the load, central to arrangement accuracy; moderate downstream consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Turn stations keep the misorientation percentage low, but marginal cases and worn turners produce a residual rate. Orientation correction happens inline so throughput is largely unaffected. Pattern recipes dictate turning automatically, reducing reliance on operator vigilance. Misoriented cases are usually caught before stacking, limiting scrap costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified layer positions left unfilled after case feeding
rows.plain: Leave fewer empty spots in the planned arrangement after placing goods into a case. This helps the goods fit securely and keeps the arrangement consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Unfilled layer positions after feeding produces incomplete/nonconforming layers, central to correct pattern building; consequences are unstable or short loads, usually caught.
rows.sat_rat: Position-count sensing catches most gaps, but upstream shortfalls still leave some layers incompletely filled. Missing-case detection can halt or hold the layer, costing some time to complete the fill. Layer-complete sensing removes the need for operators to manually count positions. Unfilled positions yield unstable or short loads that waste rework labor when they escape.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cases being dropped during feeding positioning or removal
rows.plain: Prevent cases from falling while they are being lined up for feeding or taken away. This protects the cases from damage.
rows.imp_rat: Cases dropped during feeding/positioning/removal damages product during the core placement operation; consequences are product loss and jams, moderate scale.
rows.sat_rat: Well-maintained grippers and supports make drops uncommon during feeding and removal. A dropped case interrupts flow briefly for cleanup. Secure grippers and supports mean little manual handling that could drop cases. Dropped cases are scrapped, a real but infrequent material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of empty load bases arriving outside the required feed position or orientation
rows.plain: Make sure empty load bases arrive in the correct place and facing the right way for feeding. This helps workers replenish them without extra adjustments or delays.
rows.imp_rat: Empty load bases arriving outside required feed position/orientation misaligns the foundation of the build, relevant to correct pattern formation; consequences are misbuilds and stoppage.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic pallet dispensers usually present bases correctly, but jams and skewing still occur. A misplaced empty base delays load start until corrected. Pallet dispensers position bases automatically, limiting operator handling. Misplaced bases mainly cost time, not material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case-placement pauses caused by unstable partial loads
rows.plain: Prevent pauses in placing cases when part of a load is unsteady. This helps the work continue at a steady pace.
rows.imp_rat: Case-placement pauses from unstable partial loads reduce throughput and signal stability problems, relevant to reliable stacking; consequences are delay and instability risk.
rows.sat_rat: Partial-load instability recurs with certain products, so pauses are not fully eliminated. Pauses to let unstable partial loads settle directly slow throughput. Reducing such pauses depends on pattern setup rather than added operator skill. The pauses waste line capacity rather than materials, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of damaged cases that interrupt palletizer handling during a production run
rows.plain: Prevent damaged cases from stopping the palletizing equipment during a production run. This helps production continue without interruption.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged cases interrupting palletizer handling during a run reduces reliable throughput, relevant to continuous stacking; consequences are stoppage and rework, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Upstream damage is not fully filtered, so interruptions from damaged cases persist at a moderate rate. Damaged cases jamming handling cause unplanned downtime during runs. Clearing jams from damaged cases relies on operator intervention skill. Downtime and lost cases both add cost when damage interrupts handling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of palletizing assignments staffed by operators lacking demonstrated competency in the applicable load pattern
rows.plain: Have the pallet-stacking work done by people who have shown they can follow the particular stacking arrangement. This helps prevent unstable or incorrect loads.
rows.imp_rat: Assignments staffed by operators lacking demonstrated pattern competency risks errors, relevant to reliable correct stacking; a staffing/qualification concern with moderate escaped-error stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Assignments frequently proceed on availability rather than verified competency, so competency-gap staffing is a recurring exposure. Confirming competency before assigning a pattern adds scheduling time that is often skipped under staffing pressure. The need is precisely about ensuring demonstrated skill, and today qualification tracking is patchy and not enforced at assignment. Unqualified operators produce more nonconforming pallets, an indirect but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shifts lacking enough qualified operators to cover planned palletizing work
rows.plain: Make sure every planned shift has enough properly trained workers to handle the palletizing work. This helps prevent delays and unsafe overloading of the available staff.
rows.imp_rat: Shifts lacking enough qualified operators threatens coverage of planned work, relevant to sustained operation; a staffing capacity concern with schedule/quality consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified-operator coverage is chronically tight, so shifts lacking enough qualified staff occur regularly. Short-staffed shifts run slower or leave lines idle, extending completion time. The core gap is a shortage of qualified operators to cover planned work. Coverage gaps force overtime or lost output, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pallets assigned the wrong batch identity during palletizing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of pallets given the wrong batch identity while they are being built. This helps people track each pallet back to the correct product batch.
rows.imp_rat: Pallets assigned the wrong batch identity is a traceability/data error, peripheral to physical stacking; consequences are recall complications and record errors.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/label linkage improves accuracy, but manual assignment still causes occasional errors. Wrong batch identity is usually caught in records review, adding little build time. Correct batch assignment depends on data entry and scanning rather than skill. Misassigned batch identity can trigger recalls or holds, a high cost when missed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the applicable load-pattern instructions during pallet building
rows.plain: Make the instructions for arranging goods on a pallet as clear and consistent as possible. This helps the worker build the pallet correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in load-pattern instructions during building leads to inconsistent/wrong stacking, relevant to correct arrangement; consequences are misbuilds but often clarifiable.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized pattern libraries reduce ambiguity for common products, though exceptions and updates still leave gaps. Ambiguous instructions cause hesitation and lookups that add time before building. Clear stored load-pattern recipes reduce the interpretive skill needed, but ad hoc or new patterns still demand judgement. Ambiguity leads to wrong builds and rework, a cost that documentation only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pallets whose physical case count disagrees with the batch record at load completion
rows.plain: Make sure the number of cases on each pallet matches the number written in the production record when loading is finished. This keeps the product count accurate and traceable.
rows.imp_rat: Physical case count disagreeing with the batch record is a reconciliation/traceability error, peripheral to physical stacking; consequences are inventory and record discrepancies.
rows.sat_rat: Counter integration keeps most counts aligned, but manual records still drift occasionally. Reconciling physical count to batch record is a brief step where counters are integrated. Automated counting reduces the manual reconciliation skill required. Count disagreements cause investigations and re-picks, a recurring administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of empty load bases staged with the wrong specification for scheduled cased goods
rows.plain: Have the right empty platforms ready for the products scheduled to be packed. This prevents delays and loading mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Empty load bases staged with the wrong spec for scheduled goods produces mismatched foundations, relevant to correct pattern formation; consequences are misbuilds and instability.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and staging usually match pallets to product, though errors recur occasionally. A wrong-spec load base forces a swap that interrupts building. Matching pallet spec to product depends on staging discipline rather than deep skill. Wrong pallets cause rebuilds, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an empty load-base stack shifting or toppling before use
rows.plain: Keep a stack of empty platforms from sliding or falling over before it is needed. This helps prevent damage and makes the platforms safer to use.
rows.imp_rat: An empty load-base stack shifting/toppling before use is a materials-handling safety and supply issue, relevant to base availability; toppling causes injury risk and delay.
rows.sat_rat: Pallet dispensers and standard staging keep empty stacks stable most of the time. A toppling empty pallet stack interrupts staging and costs time to restack. Stable empty-stack staging relies on basic material-handling practice, not scarce skill. Toppled stacks damage pallets and delay work, a small cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of incoming cases becoming obstructed before reaching the required placement
rows.plain: Prevent incoming cases from getting blocked before they reach the place where they need to go. This helps keep the flow of work moving without stoppages.
rows.imp_rat: Incoming cases obstructed before reaching placement disrupts feed continuity, relevant to reliable stacking throughput; consequences are stoppage and misfeeds, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Conveyor guides and photoeyes reduce obstructions, yet they still recur enough to be a routine nuisance. Obstructions before placement stall the feed and add clearing time. Clearing obstructions is routine but needs an attentive operator to spot them. Obstructed cases are usually recovered so material loss is limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in separation between consecutive cases approaching the load-forming point
rows.plain: Keep the space between one case and the next consistent as they move toward the loading point. This helps each case reach the right position for handling.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in separation between consecutive cases approaching load forming affects placement timing and accuracy, relevant to reliable feed; consequences are misplacement, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Gapping conveyors keep separation fairly consistent, though variation persists at the load-forming point. Inconsistent separation near load forming can trigger brief holds. Metering and gapping devices reduce reliance on manual pacing. Separation variation risks collisions but rarely direct scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in arrival spacing between cases entering the palletizer
rows.plain: Keep the gaps between cases arriving at the pallet-building machine consistent. This helps the machine handle cases smoothly without slowing down or stopping.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in arrival spacing between cases disrupts orderly feed and placement timing, relevant to reliable layer formation; consequences are misplacement and jams, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation and metering conveyors control spacing reasonably, but upstream surges still create variation. Uneven arrival spacing can cause stalls that intermittently slow feeding. Metering belts handle spacing automatically, limiting operator involvement. Spacing variation mainly risks jams rather than direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of failure resolution caused by conflicting shippability criteria among manufacturing, warehousing, carriers, and recipients
rows.plain: Resolve failed loads more quickly when the groups involved disagree about what can be shipped. This helps avoid delays while deciding whether the load is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged resolution from conflicting shippability criteria among parties delays recovery, relevant to timely restoration; a coordination/governance concern with indirect stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Absent a single agreed standard, criteria conflicts recur and stall every related investigation. Reconciling conflicting shippability criteria among plants, warehouses, carriers and recipients drags resolution out for weeks. Navigating and aligning multiple parties' standards demands cross-functional negotiation skill the role rarely holds. Prolonged disputes tie up held inventory and staff time at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of load-failure events with incomplete pattern, case, pallet, handling, or rejection evidence
rows.plain: Make sure each load failure has complete evidence about the pattern, cases, pallet, handling, and rejected items, so the cause can be understood.
rows.imp_rat: Load-failure events with incomplete evidence hinders root-cause resolution, relevant to eliminating recurring failures; a diagnostic/documentation gap with indirect stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Load-failure events are commonly recorded with partial or ad hoc evidence, undermining reliable diagnosis. Missing evidence forces investigators to wait for the next failure to gather what they needed. Knowing what pattern, case and handling data to capture takes investigative expertise that is often absent at the point of failure. Incomplete evidence prolongs holds and wrong fixes, inflating investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejecting a stable completed load as unstable during verification
rows.plain: Avoid rejecting a finished load that is actually stable when checking whether it can remain safely in place.
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting a stable load as unstable is a false-positive verification error causing unnecessary rework; relevant to efficient verification but consequences are cost/delay only.
rows.sat_rat: Without objective stability metrics, conservative operators reject good loads intermittently, so consistency is only fair. False rejection triggers unnecessary rebuilds that waste line time. Judging a stable load as unstable reflects imprecise, judgement-based criteria rather than objective measurement. Rebuilding good loads wastes labor and materials, a recurring avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected cases whose recorded pallet assignment differs from their physical pallet
rows.plain: Reduce cases being recorded as belonging to one pallet when they are physically on another, so tracking and handling remain accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded pallet assignment differing from physical pallet is a traceability/data error, peripheral to the physical stacking CFJ; consequences are inventory and recall complications.
rows.sat_rat: Manual or partial scanning means physical-to-record mismatches persist, particularly after rework or manual pallet swaps. Recording assignment via scan is near-instant when done. Correct pallet-record matching depends on disciplined scanning rather than deep skill. Mismatched records cause traceability investigations and misships, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of rejecting completed loads that meet the applicable stacking specification
rows.plain: Reduce how often finished loads are rejected even though they meet the required stacking rules. This avoids unnecessary rework, delay, and waste.
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting conforming completed loads is a false-positive causing needless rework and waste; relevant to verification accuracy but consequences are cost/delay, not harm.
rows.sat_rat: Subjective acceptance criteria cause inconsistent rejection of compliant loads, leaving this modestly served. Unnecessary rejection of conforming loads adds rebuild time to the run. Distinguishing conforming from non-conforming loads depends on inspector interpretation of the spec. Rejecting good loads wastes materials and labor without benefit, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cased product requiring rework or disposal because of recovery damage
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of packaged product that must be repaired or thrown away because it was damaged while being recovered from a disruption. This saves usable product and reduces waste.
rows.imp_rat: Product requiring rework/disposal due to recovery damage is a waste/cost outcome from exception handling, peripheral to the core stacking job; consequences are material loss.
rows.sat_rat: Damage during recovery is erratic and poorly contained, so the amount needing disposal varies widely event to event. Rework and disposal after recovery consume time but the driver is the wasted product itself. Sorting salvageable from scrap during recovery leans on experienced judgement. Recovery episodes generate concentrated batches of damaged product that go to rework or disposal, a notable recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among case-damage and load-instability criteria used by packaging, production, distribution, and receiving stakeholders
rows.plain: Make sure the rules used by different groups for preventing case damage and unstable loads do not conflict. Consistent rules help everyone prepare, move, and receive shipments safely.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among damage/instability criteria across stakeholders creates inconsistent acceptance, relevant to coherent conformance definition; a governance concern with indirect stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting criteria among packaging, production, distribution, and receiving are common and poorly reconciled in status-quo practice. Reconciling stakeholder criteria takes drawn-out cross-functional discussion that delays specification. Resolving conflicting damage and stability criteria across four functions demands negotiation and judgement rarely concentrated in one role. Unresolved criteria conflicts produce loads that satisfy one group and cost another in damage or claims.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of load-forming interruption caused by a format transition
rows.plain: Shorten interruptions when the equipment changes from making one package type to another. This helps production restart sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Load-forming interruption duration from a format transition is a changeover-efficiency metric, relevant to throughput continuity; consequences are downtime, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Transition interruptions are predictable but still lengthy on many machines. Format transitions impose real load-forming downtime while the machine reconfigures. Guided changeover menus reduce but do not remove operator effort. Transition downtime is lost production capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of adjustments required before the incoming configuration stacks consistently
rows.plain: Reduce the number of changes needed before the incoming setup stacks items evenly and reliably. This helps the changeover finish faster.
rows.imp_rat: Adjustments required before an incoming configuration stacks consistently reflects setup inefficiency, relevant to reliable stacking; consequences are delay and early misbuilds, recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Getting a new configuration to stack consistently often takes several iterative corrections. Repeated adjustments before a stable stack consume setup time. Achieving consistent stacking still demands experienced tuning of guides and parameters. Trial adjustments waste product and labor during setup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unsafe palletizing deviations recurring after being classified as resolved
rows.plain: Reduce how often unsafe mistakes in stacking products on pallets happen again after they were marked as fixed. This helps ensure the correction continues to work over time.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring unsafe deviations after being marked resolved is a safety-follow-through gap (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Deviations recurring after being marked resolved is a well-known chronic failure of corrective action. Recurrence is detected only at later reviews, extending exposure time. Verifying that a deviation is truly resolved requires root-cause skill often absent. Repeated deviations waste corrective-action resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of case accumulation caused by palletizing below the required pace
rows.plain: Shorten the time that packages pile up because they are being stacked more slowly than required. This helps the work keep moving at the needed pace.
rows.imp_rat: Case accumulation from slow palletizing directly relates to pace (relevance) but recoverable (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Rated palletizer speed usually keeps pace, but slowdowns still cause accumulation at times. Palletizing below required pace causes case accumulation that costs throughput. Pace is machine-governed, not skill-limited. Accumulation ties up buffer capacity, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned stoppages caused by palletizer faults during production runs
rows.plain: Reduce unexpected stops caused by faults in the pallet-stacking machine during production. This helps keep the flow of work going.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages from faults cut stacking output (relevance) with recoverable downtime (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical palletizers still fault periodically, so unplanned stoppages remain a recurring pain. Unplanned stoppages from faults directly consume production time. Fault recovery often needs technician skill, a real dependence. Downtime and maintenance labor are the costs of faults.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of case jams between line discharge and pallet placement
rows.plain: Have fewer shipping cases get stuck between leaving the production line and being placed on a pallet. This keeps products moving without interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Jams between discharge and pallet placement interrupt stacking (relevance) but are quickly cleared (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Transfer jams occur intermittently with certain case types, so consistency is moderate. Jams between discharge and placement stall the cycle, costing time. Clearing these jams needs operator attention, not scarce skill. Downtime is the cost of these jams.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an empty pallet being unavailable when the next load must begin
rows.plain: Make sure an empty pallet is ready before the next load needs to start. This prevents the flow of work from stopping.
rows.imp_rat: No empty pallet when the next load begins stalls stacking (relevance) but is a recoverable supply issue (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automatic pallet dispensers keep empties available most of the time. An unavailable empty pallet stalls the next load, directly costing throughput time. Pallet availability is a logistics task needing little skill. Waiting time is the main cost when pallets run short.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verified loads altered before discharge from the palletizing area
rows.plain: Fewer loads that have already been checked should be changed before they leave the palletizing area. This helps ensure that the load leaving the area is still as safe and stable as when it was checked.
rows.imp_rat: Verified loads altered before discharge invalidates the check (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Few controls guard against changes after verification, so this remains a gap. Detecting post-verification alteration adds little time but is rarely done. Preventing alteration relies on procedural control rather than skill. Altered verified loads can ship non-conforming, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prior-batch cases remaining in the palletizing area after a batch transition
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cases from the previous product group that remain in the packing area after changing to a new group. This makes it easier to tell which group each case belongs to.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch cases in the area after transition risks mixing (relevance) with significant traceability consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Clearance procedures reduce leftovers but are imperfectly executed. Prior-batch cases left after transition require clearing that costs time. Line clearance is procedural, not skill-intensive. Leftover cases risk mixing and rework, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of diverted cases being returned to the wrong batch grouping during palletizing
rows.plain: Make sure cases moved aside are returned to the correct group when they are placed on a pallet. This helps keep each product group accurately tracked.
rows.imp_rat: Diverted cases returned to wrong batch grouping corrupts load identity (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual handling of diverts makes wrong-grouping returns a recurring risk. Returning diverted cases to the wrong grouping causes corrections that cost time. Correct return relies on operator tracking of diverted cases. Misgrouped returns cause mixing and rework, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing a case-to-pallet association before load completion
rows.plain: Keep each case linked to the correct pallet until the pallet is complete. This helps people know exactly where each case belongs and which product group it came from.
rows.imp_rat: Losing case-to-pallet association before completion harms traceability (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where automated tracking exists association is stable, but manual systems lose it intermittently. Losing case-to-pallet association forces re-verification that costs time. rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel misinterpret palletizer safety status during a fault or intervention
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that people misunderstand whether the equipment is safe during a problem or repair, which could lead them into danger.
rows.imp_rat: Misinterpreting safety status is a communication gap peripheral to stacking (relevance) with meaningful injury risk (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Status signaling is standard but not uniformly intuitive, so occasional misinterpretation during faults persists. Misread status can cause hesitation or premature action that costs intervention time. Correctly interpreting safety status during a fault leans on operator familiarity with indicators that are not always clear. Status indicators are cheap, so the gap is comprehension rather than hardware cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of palletizing periods with required personnel-protection controls bypassed
rows.plain: Have fewer periods of pallet building when required protections for people have been bypassed. This reduces the risk of someone being hurt.
rows.imp_rat: Bypassed protection controls during palletizing is a safety concern peripheral to stacking (relevance) but dangerous (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Control bypassing still occurs episodically despite policy, and detection of active bypasses is often weak. Bypassing controls to keep running saves short-term time but is exactly the risky shortcut at issue. Recognizing and refusing improper bypasses depends on safety culture and supervision more than technical skill. Bypass hardware costs little; the exposure is the human tendency to defeat controls under production pressure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned palletizer stoppages during scheduled production
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected stops in the machine that groups products onto pallets during planned production. Fewer stops help production keep moving and meet its schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages during production directly cut stacking output (relevance) but are recoverable downtime (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Preventive maintenance reduces stoppages, but jams and faults during runs remain a common recurring event. Unplanned stoppages interrupt scheduled production and require reactive restarts that cost time. Preventing and quickly clearing stoppages depends on maintenance and operator troubleshooting skill. Downtime carries real cost, mitigated by preventive maintenance programs of varying rigor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of running production intervals with pallet formation below the required output target
rows.plain: Keep production from falling below its required output while loads are being built on pallets. This helps the operation produce the expected amount on time.
rows.imp_rat: Output below target directly touches the throughput aspect of forming loads (relevance) but the effect is lost production, not catastrophic (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Palletizer output is reasonably consistent when well maintained, with occasional dips from upstream disruption. Below-target formation intervals directly reduce output, but standard rate monitoring flags them fairly promptly. Keeping formation at target depends on operator tuning and troubleshooting skill that experienced crews handle competently. Lost throughput has a cost, though line-balancing practices keep chronic shortfalls limited.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of palletizer idling caused by interrupted case infeed
rows.plain: Reduce the time the pallet-stacking machine sits idle when boxes stop arriving. This helps maintain the planned output.
rows.imp_rat: Idling from interrupted infeed reduces throughput of stacking (relevance) but recoverable (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Buffer conveyors and infeed controls are standard and keep idling from minor upstream hiccups fairly contained. Infeed interruptions cause idling, but accumulation conveyors buffer many short gaps. Managing infeed flow is largely automated, needing little operator intervention. Idle time is lost throughput, partly absorbed by buffering at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of palletizer unavailability during product or load-pattern changeovers
rows.plain: Reduce the time the pallet-stacking machine cannot be used while changing to a different product or stacking arrangement. This helps production resume sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability during changeovers reduces stacking output (relevance) but is planned/recoverable (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Stored pattern recipes make changeovers repeatable, but variability and manual steps still cause avoidable delay. Changeover downtime for new products or load patterns is a well-known but still substantial time loss. Efficient changeovers depend on skilled setup of pattern programs and tooling that varies by operator. Changeover downtime is lost capacity with a recognized cost, partly mitigated by recipe storage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a scheduled palletizing run starts without enough acceptable empty pallets
rows.plain: Avoid starting a planned pallet-loading run unless there are enough good, empty pallets available. This helps prevent delays and interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Starting without enough acceptable pallets stalls stacking (relevance) but is a recoverable supply issue (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Pallet supply is normally staged ahead of runs, though poor-quality empties occasionally cause interruptions. Running out of acceptable empty pallets stops a run until replenished, a delay usually avoided by staging. Pallet staging and quality screening are routine tasks requiring little specialized skill. Staging enough good pallets is a low-cost logistics practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of palletizing holds caused by conflicting production-order and load-pattern requirements
rows.plain: Have fewer pauses in pallet building because the production order and the planned way of stacking the load do not agree. This helps keep production moving and prevents rework.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting order/pattern holds interrupt load forming somewhat (relevance) but consequences are recoverable delays (severity), keeping importance modest.
rows.sat_rat: Order and pattern data are often maintained separately, so mismatches surface unpredictably during runs. Holds from conflicting production-order and load-pattern requirements stop the line until someone resolves the mismatch. Spotting and reconciling order-versus-pattern conflicts relies on planners' and operators' familiarity rather than a system check. Each hold wastes running capacity, but the events are intermittent rather than constant.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases whose required identification marks are obscured or incorrectly oriented in the specified unit load
rows.plain: Fewer cases should have required labels or markings hidden or facing the wrong way on the specified pallet load. This helps people identify and handle the load correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Obscured identification marks affect load usability (relevance) with correctable rework consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Identification-mark orientation is sometimes considered in pattern design but is inconsistently enforced, leaving recurring obscured-label cases. Checking label visibility against a pattern is a quick visual or software step. Anticipating obscured or misoriented marks requires attention but modest specialized skill. Obscured marks cause scanning failures and rehandling costs partly avoided today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical product-protection and load-stability requirements omitted from the evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Fewer important requirements for protecting the product and keeping the load stable should be left out of the judging criteria. This helps ensure proposed solutions protect both the product and the load.
rows.imp_rat: Missing product-protection/stability criteria weakens the load quality definition (relevance) causing recoverable defects (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Evaluation criteria commonly omit some critical protection or stability requirement, a persistent and consequential gap. Assembling complete criteria takes upfront effort often truncated under time pressure. Knowing which product-protection and stability requirements are critical demands broad experience seldom fully present. Omitted critical requirements surface as field damage whose cost dwarfs the effort to include them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failure of a critical product-protection or load-stability criterion is masked by favorable lower-priority results
rows.plain: Do not let good results in less important areas hide a failure in a requirement that protects the product or keeps the load stable. A serious failure in either of those areas must remain clear in the evaluation.
rows.imp_rat: Masked criterion failures can let bad loads pass (relevance) with meaningful quality escape consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo scoring rarely isolates critical criteria, so a failed stability or protection requirement can be masked by favorable overall results. Detecting masked critical failures requires extra scrutiny that slows evaluation. Recognizing when a good aggregate result hides a critical failure requires sophisticated evaluation design most lack. Masked critical failures escape to the field where they cost far more than at evaluation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the density-optimized load weight exceeds the allowable limit
rows.plain: Keep the loaded weight from going over the allowed maximum, even when the load is packed to use space efficiently.
rows.imp_rat: Density-optimized weight exceeding limit affects handling safety and load acceptance (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Weight limits are hard numeric constraints routinely enforced, so density-driven overweight is a rare, well-controlled failure. Weight-limit checks on density-optimized loads are instant computations. Comparing computed load weight to an allowable limit requires minimal skill. Overweight loads risk fines, but the cheap automated check largely prevents overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required case identification is obscured by a density-optimized arrangement
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that arranging boxes tightly makes their required identifying marks hard to see or verify.
rows.imp_rat: Identification obscured by dense arrangement affects load usability (relevance) with correctable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Density optimization routinely competes with label visibility, and status-quo practice inconsistently protects required identification. Verifying label exposure on a dense pattern adds minor evaluation time. Reconciling density with label visibility takes judgement but not deep expertise. Density-driven label obstruction causes scan failures and manual handling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory sterile-boundary, contamination-control, or regulatory requirements omitted from the assessment
rows.plain: Fewer required rules for keeping products free from contamination, protecting enclosed clean areas, or meeting official standards should be left out of the review. This helps ensure the chosen approach is safe and compliant.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting mandatory sterile/regulatory requirements from assessment is relevant to compliant loads (relevance) with serious consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature pharma regulatory checklists catch most mandatory requirements, yet edge cases around palletizing boundaries still slip through. Regulatory requirement capture leans on established GMP checklists, which speeds but does not fully cover the assessment. Sterile-boundary and contamination-control expertise is available through QA/regulatory staff, though their involvement in procurement is inconsistent. Omitted regulatory requirements surface as costly late remediation, partially mitigated by standard compliance frameworks.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible personnel hazards during palletizing, intervention, or fault recovery absent from the assessment
rows.plain: Fewer realistic dangers to workers should be left out when reviewing palletizing, hands-on fixes, or recovery from equipment problems. This helps protect people in both normal and unusual situations.
rows.imp_rat: Credible personnel hazards absent from assessment is a safety-completeness gap (relevance) with serious consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature safety-assessment methods catch most credible palletizing hazards, though intervention and recovery states are sometimes underexplored. Hazard identification uses established risk-assessment sessions that fit within project schedules. Machine-safety expertise and HAZOP facilitators are widely available for this type of equipment. Missed hazards drive costly incidents and retrofits, but standard safety practice reduces this materially.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an approach judged acceptable compromises batch identity, production records, or status segregation
rows.plain: Make sure a method that seems acceptable does not mix up the product, its records, or its status. This helps keep each batch correctly identified and separated from others.
rows.imp_rat: Approach compromising batch identity/records is relevant to correct loads (relevance) with significant traceability consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Segregation logic is addressed in serialization systems but its interaction with palletizing controls is inconsistently verified. Checking batch-identity and status-segregation impacts is a manual review that slows sign-off. Judging whether a control approach preserves batch identity and segregation needs specialized GMP data-integrity knowledge. A lapse here risks batch loss, a severe cost the current review only partially guards against.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of palletizer movement creating a personnel hazard during access, stoppage, or recovery states
rows.plain: Make sure the palletizing equipment cannot move in a way that could hurt someone when people enter, stop the equipment, or restart it. This helps keep access and recovery activities safe.
rows.imp_rat: Movement creating personnel hazards during access is peripheral to stacking (relevance) with serious injury consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Category-rated safety circuits reliably prevent hazardous movement in most access and stoppage states. Assessing movement hazards during access and recovery is embedded in standard safety validation. Functional-safety competency for hazardous motion is well established among integrators. Safe-motion controls carry a known cost that is well justified against harm.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable quality, traceability, and contamination-control obligations omitted from the evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Include every relevant requirement for product quality, tracking, and preventing contamination in the evaluation. Leaving one out could allow an important risk to be missed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted quality/traceability obligations weakens criteria (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard quality checklists cover the common obligations but contamination-control specifics for load formation are often incompletely mapped. Traceability and quality obligations are pulled from existing SOP libraries reasonably quickly. Quality staff know the obligations but translating them into evaluation criteria for palletizing controls is not routine skill. Missing obligations create audit-finding costs that established quality systems only partly prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of loads formed outside defined arrangement, stability, or case-integrity limits under required conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the number of loads that are arranged incorrectly, are unstable, or have damaged boxes under required conditions. This helps loads remain safe and intact while being handled.
rows.imp_rat: Loads formed outside stability/integrity limits directly touches the arranging job (relevance) with meaningful defect consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern palletizer controls and test protocols keep most loads within limits, though disrupted conditions still produce failures. Load-pattern conformance is verified during FAT/SAT runs that take defined time but are well established. Pattern programming and validation is a maturing engineering competency supported by vendor tools. Out-of-limit loads cause product damage and rejects, partly contained by acceptance testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved hazards to operators, maintenance personnel, or nearby staff around pallet formation
rows.plain: Fewer safety dangers should remain for workers, repair staff, or people nearby while pallets are being built. This helps prevent injuries during pallet stacking.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved hazards around pallet formation is a safety-management gap (relevance) with serious consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-deployed machine-safety standards resolve most operator and maintenance hazards, leaving few unresolved. Resolving identified hazards follows a known safety-engineering workflow with predictable duration. Safety engineers routinely apply guarding, interlocks and safe-access standards to pallet formation. Standard safeguarding is a budgeted cost that reliably reduces expensive injury exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evaluated case types with inaccurate weight, dimensions, stability, or handling characteristics
rows.plain: Make sure the listed weight, size, strength, and handling needs of each case type are accurate. This helps people choose arrangements that can be moved and stored safely.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate case characteristics affects correct pattern building (relevance) with meaningful defect consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Inaccurate case characteristics are common because data quality control is weak. Verifying case data accuracy is a data-collection task done before assessment. Accurate weight and stability characterization requires measurement skill and good source data. Measurement adds some cost but is not the primary loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of case or pallet repositioning required after a palletizer stop
rows.imp_rat: Repositioning after a stop is rework tied to stacking (relevance) with minor consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Restart repositioning is common after stops, so this recurs regularly. Repositioning cases or pallets after a stop directly adds recovery time. Recovery repositioning relies on operator intervention rather than automation. The time lost is the main cost, with little material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed loads with required case labels obscured by the stacking arrangement
rows.imp_rat: Labels obscured by stacking affects load usability (relevance) with correctable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Label-out patterns are common practice, but some patterns still obscure labels. Obscured labels are usually found downstream, so palletizing time impact is small. Pattern design determines label visibility, not operator skill. Obscured labels cause downstream re-handling, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cases with missing or unreadable batch identifiers at palletizer infeed
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable batch IDs at infeed affect traceability (relevance) with correctable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scanner read rates are high but not perfect, so unreadable identifiers still appear. An unreadable identifier at infeed can hold a case, adding time. Reading identifiers is automated by scanners, reducing operator skill dependence. Unreadable identifiers cause holds and manual keying, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of staged cases with unreadable or conflicting identity information before palletizing
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable/conflicting identity on staged cases affects sorting into loads (relevance) with correctable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data quality varies upstream, so conflicting identity information recurs at a moderate rate. Conflicting identity info before palletizing forces resolution that costs time. Resolving conflicting identity data needs operator judgement to reconcile sources. Unresolved identity issues cause holds and rework, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of empty load bases staged outside the required palletizer pickup position
rows.imp_rat: Bases staged outside pickup position disrupts stacking flow (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Dispensers and fixtures keep bases positioned consistently. Bases staged outside pickup position force repositioning that costs time. Correct positioning is a staging task needing little skill. Repositioning wastes minor labor time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of load-base replenishment delays caused by blocked palletizer access
rows.imp_rat: Replenishment delays from blocked access interrupt stacking (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Layout and staging usually keep access clear, with occasional blockages. Blocked access delaying base replenishment idles the palletizer. Access management is a housekeeping task, not a skill. Delay time is the main cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of incoming cases with unreadable or conflicting identity markings at the load-forming point
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable identity at load-forming point affects grouping (relevance) with correctable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cases with unreadable or conflicting markings regularly reach load forming with poor upstream screening. Conflicting markings force resolution pauses. Interpreting bad markings depends on operator effort. Unreadable identity risks misrouting and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed loads requiring teardown because of batch-grouping discrepancies
rows.imp_rat: Loads torn down for batch-grouping discrepancies is rework tied to stacking (relevance) with contained consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Batch-grouping discrepancies requiring teardown recur because grouping is not systematically enforced. Teardowns for batch grouping consume significant rework time. Preventing grouping errors relies on operator batch tracking. Teardown and rebuild is expensive lost labor and product handling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of case walls or corners punctured during load formation
rows.imp_rat: Punctured case walls during formation affects product integrity (relevance) with contained consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Rounded guides and smooth surfaces keep puncture damage relatively uncommon. Punctured cases require removal, briefly stopping flow. Smooth tooling limits the operator care needed to avoid punctures. Punctured cases are scrap, a low-level cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of case closures opening during load formation
rows.imp_rat: Case closures opening during formation affects load integrity (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Case closures generally hold during forming, with occasional openings on weak seals. Opened closures interrupt flow for handling. Gentle handling reduces the skill needed to prevent openings. Opened cases spill product and add cleanup cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a stability result is linked to the wrong completed load
rows.imp_rat: Stability result linked to wrong load undermines verification (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Stability disposition tracking is weak, so results are sometimes mapped to the wrong load. Mislinked stability results cause later confusion and delay. Manual recording links stability judgements to loads by hand. Misassigned stability results risk shipping unstable loads.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stability verification damages cases or compromises package integrity
rows.imp_rat: Stability verification damaging cases affects load integrity (relevance) with correctable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual and light-touch stability checks seldom compromise package integrity. Stability checks add minor time and rarely damage cases. Careful checking limits damage but depends on operator technique. Damage from verification is an occasional minor scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that a completed load matches the defined pattern and case count at confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about pattern/count match is a verification-confidence issue (relevance) with modest consequence (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended case, pallet, and shipment configurations for which evaluation criteria are undefined
rows.imp_rat: Undefined evaluation criteria for configurations is a specification gap (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: A significant share of case, pallet, and shipment configurations lack defined evaluation criteria, leaving them effectively unchecked. Defining criteria for every configuration is slow and frequently deferred. Determining appropriate criteria for each configuration needs specialist input that is thinly spread. Undefined criteria mean unevaluated loads that generate downstream damage cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between criterion weights and sterile fill-finish quality, safety, continuity, and shipment priorities
rows.imp_rat: Criterion-weight mismatch with sterile priorities is peripheral to stacking (relevance) with moderate quality consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Without a standardized pharma-specific weighting rubric the outcome varies by team and mismatch persists undetected. Aligning criterion weights to fill-finish priorities is done in weighting workshops that add weeks but rarely resolve the mismatch quickly. This demands scarce cross-functional judgement spanning aseptic quality, engineering, and logistics that few evaluators hold simultaneously, so weightings often reflect whoever dominates the room. Misweighted criteria are cheap to write but drive expensive wrong recommendations later, and today no tooling catches the drift.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that infrequent high-consequence handling hazards are ranked below routine lower-consequence hazards
rows.imp_rat: Misranking high-consequence hazards below routine ones is a prioritization flaw (relevance) with real safety consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: High-consequence hazards are frequently under-ranked due to frequency-biased scoring. Ranking hazards is a bounded analytical step. Properly weighting rare high-consequence hazards against routine ones demands sophisticated risk judgement often lacking. Ranking costs little but misranking drives misallocated mitigation spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of worker-exposure hazards at case infeed, pallet exchange, and completed-load removal excluded from prioritization
rows.imp_rat: Worker-exposure hazards excluded from prioritization is a safety-planning gap (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Hazards at these zones are inconsistently included in prioritization, leaving gaps. Prioritizing infeed, exchange, and removal hazards is part of periodic assessment cycles. Comprehensive hazard prioritization across all interaction zones requires broad expertise. Prioritization work is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of expected case configurations excluded from the safety evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Case configurations excluded from safety evaluation is a coverage gap (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Expected configurations are frequently excluded, undermining evaluation completeness. Enumerating case configurations is a scoping step in the evaluation. Knowing the full population of case configurations requires product-data expertise. Adding configurations to scope is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of loads requiring manual rearrangement because specified patterns conflict with handling or transport constraints
rows.imp_rat: Loads needing manual rearrangement from pattern conflicts is rework tied to stacking (relevance) with minor consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Pattern-constraint conflicts recur when transport limits are not reconciled at design. Manual rearrangement adds handling time but is not the primary loss. Rearranging conflicting loads is low-skill but repetitive labor. Loads needing manual rearrangement waste labor and materials, making cost the dominant concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which evaluated case formats, load patterns, and shipment conditions differ from intended-use conditions
rows.imp_rat: Evaluated conditions differing from intended use weakens validity (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Evaluated conditions frequently diverge from intended use, reducing conclusion validity. Comparing evaluated to intended-use conditions is a scoping check. Matching evaluated conditions to real use requires knowledge of actual shipment conditions. The comparison is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which assessed patterns exclude actual variation in case dimensions, mass, rigidity, or surface condition
rows.imp_rat: Assessed patterns excluding real case variation weakens the load spec (relevance) with moderate consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Assessed patterns routinely omit real-world case variation, degrading reliability of conclusions. Capturing case variation is done at assessment setup. Characterizing variation in dimension, mass, and rigidity requires measurement discipline. Gathering variation data has modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of palletizing near-misses and unsafe handling deviations omitted from compliance evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Near-misses omitted from compliance evaluation is a safety-completeness gap (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: A large share of near-misses go unreported, so compliance evaluation is systematically incomplete. Near-misses are often logged late or never, delaying their entry into evaluation. Recognizing and reporting near-misses depends on a safety culture and skill not uniformly present. Capturing near-misses is cheap relative to the incidents they predict.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that temporary safe handling conditions are classified as sustained palletizing compliance
rows.imp_rat: Temporary conditions misclassified as sustained compliance is an assessment-validity gap (relevance) with meaningful consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Temporary conditions are often mistaken for sustained compliance, a recurring reliability error. Distinguishing temporary from sustained compliance requires observation over time that is often skipped. Judging sustainability of compliance requires disciplined trend analysis. Extended observation adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed loads with incomplete or mismatched conformance records
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete conformance records is a documentation defect (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual record-keeping leaves recurring gaps, so mismatched records persist. Incomplete conformance records require follow-up that delays release, costing time. Record completeness depends on documentation discipline rather than skill. Missing records cause holds and rework, an administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of load interfaces that cannot be assessed before discharge from the palletizing area
rows.imp_rat: Unassessable load interfaces before discharge is a verification-coverage gap (relevance) with modest consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: No standard method reveals internal interfaces, so this is poorly served. Load interfaces that can't be assessed before discharge force delays or accept-blind decisions. Assessing hidden interfaces exceeds what visual inspection can provide. Unassessed interfaces lead to transit failures, a costly gap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed loads with missing or mismatched stability results
rows.imp_rat: Missing stability results is a records defect (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Stability documentation is inconsistent where it is manual, leaving real gaps. Missing stability results delay release while they are reconstructed. Capturing stability results depends on procedure adherence, not deep skill. Missing results cause holds and re-inspection, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time cases wait at the pattern entry before being fed into a layer
rows.imp_rat: Cases waiting at pattern entry is a minor pacing issue (relevance) with trivial consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Buffering keeps entry waits consistent, though pace mismatches lengthen them at times. Cases wait at pattern entry when the layer table is busy, adding queue time. Queue management is automatic, needing little operator input. Waiting time is modest lost throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a completed load waits in the build area after becoming ready for removal
rows.imp_rat: Completed load waiting in build area is a minor flow issue (relevance) with trivial consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Discharge cycles are fairly consistent, but downstream backups create occasional waits. Ready loads sometimes wait in the build area for removal, delaying the next load. Removal is automated, needing little skill. Build-area waits cost some throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of conforming cases mistakenly removed from load-forming flow
rows.imp_rat: Conforming cases mistakenly removed is a false-reject defect (relevance) with recoverable consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conforming cases are occasionally pulled by mistake during manual segregation. Removing good cases needlessly adds handling time. Distinguishing conforming from nonconforming leans on operator judgement. Wrongly removed good cases waste product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a completed load waits for stability disposition after palletizing
rows.imp_rat: Wait for stability disposition is a minor release delay (relevance) with trivial consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Stability disposition is inconsistently timed, causing recurring waits after palletizing. Loads wait for stability disposition when it depends on manual judgement. Stability disposition relies on an available skilled operator. Waiting loads occupy build space and delay flow.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a pattern-conforming load is rejected as nonconforming
rows.imp_rat: Conforming load wrongly rejected is rework/false-reject (relevance) with minor consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Subjective checks cause some conforming loads to be wrongly rejected, though this is less common than escapes. False rejection triggers unnecessary teardown time. Over-cautious operators reject good loads based on subjective judgement. Rejecting conforming loads wastes rework labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a completed load waits for pattern-conformance confirmation before release
rows.imp_rat: Wait for pattern-conformance confirmation is a minor release-flow delay (relevance) with trivial consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because verification is manual and not always prompt, loads routinely wait for confirmation. Loads wait for pattern-conformance confirmation when checks are manual and queued. Manual confirmation depends on an available operator. Confirmation delays tie up build-area capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of distinct palletizing failure modes grouped under a common symptom
rows.imp_rat: Failure modes grouped under one symptom is a diagnostic-clarity issue (relevance) with modest consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Coarse symptom-based logging routinely lumps different failure modes together, undermining reliable diagnosis. Grouping failures under one symptom delays true diagnosis and prolongs repeat downtime. Distinguishing distinct failure modes behind one symptom demands analytical skill rarely present on the floor. Misgrouped failures drive wasted fixes and recurring losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether palletizing pace can sustain downstream demand
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about sustaining downstream pace is a planning-visibility issue (relevance) with minor consequence (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the time completed pallets wait for discharge clearance
rows.imp_rat: Wait time for discharge clearance is a minor flow-efficiency issue (relevance) with trivial consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Discharge conveyors and forklift routines are standard and keep wait-for-clearance events fairly rare and brief. Completed pallets occasionally wait for discharge clearance, but takeaway conveyors keep waits short. Discharge flow is automated and needs minimal operator skill to manage. Waiting pallets tie up little beyond floor space at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among quality, carrier, customer, and destination requirements in the evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved requirement conflicts in criteria is a specification gap (relevance) with modest consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting requirements across parties frequently persist unresolved in the criteria used to evaluate loads. Resolving cross-party requirement conflicts drags on through slow multi-stakeholder coordination. Reconciling quality, carrier, customer, and destination demands requires seasoned negotiation and technical judgement. Unresolved conflicts produce loads that fail one party's requirements, driving claims and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of avoidable void space within the usable load envelope
rows.imp_rat: Avoidable void space is an efficiency/density concern (relevance) with minor consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cube-optimization algorithms consistently reduce void space within the envelope for standard cases. Void-space calculation is instantaneous in modern pattern software. Minimizing void space is largely automated, needing little manual skill. Void space directly wastes cube and freight cost, and optimization tools address this well.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts among production, quality, safety, and shipment constraints embedded in the control requirements
rows.imp_rat: Embedded constraint conflicts is a requirements-coherence issue (relevance) with modest consequence (severity).
